slides.title: Up next Solving Linear Inequalities (One Variable)
slides.html:
01Up next

Solving Linear Inequalities (One Variable)

slides.title: What is a Linear Inequality?
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

A linear inequality is similar to a linear equation, but instead of using \(=\), it uses:

  • \(\lt \) (less than)
  • \(\le\) (less than or equal to)
  • \(>\) (greater than)
  • \(\ge\) (greater than or equal to)

Examples:

\[x + 2 \lt 5 \text{or} 2x - 3 \ge 7\]
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Solving Linear Inequalities (One Variable)
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

Steps:

  1. Isolate the variable on one side.
  2. Use inverse operations as with equations.
  3. Important: Flip the inequality sign when multiplying or dividing by a negative number!

Example:

\[ -3x + 2 \le 8 ⇒ -3x \le 6 ⇒ x \ge -2 \]
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Graphing One-Variable Inequalities
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

  • Use an open circle for \(\lt \) or \(>\).
  • Use a closed circle for \(\le\) or \(\ge\).
  • Shade the direction of the inequality.

Example: \(x > 2\) is represented with an open circle at \(2\) and shading to the right.
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Up next Linear Inequalities in Two Variables
slides.html:
02Up next

Linear Inequalities in Two Variables

slides.title: Linear Inequalities in Two Variables
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

A linear inequality in two variables looks like:

\[Ax + By \lt C \text{or} Ax + By \ge C\]

Steps to graph:

  1. Rewrite as an equation to find the boundary line.
  2. Use a solid line for \(\le\) or \(\ge\), dashed for \(\lt \) or \(>\).
  3. Pick a test point (usually \((0,0)\)) to determine the shading side.
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Example: Graphing y < 2x + 1
slides.html:
Strategy

Mark known angles and side relationships first, then use triangle or parallel-line facts.

Guided ExampleWatch the process

Follow the model first, then try the next similar problem yourself.

  • Step 1: Graph the boundary line \(y = 2x + 1\) (dashed line).
  • Step 2: Test \((0,0)\): \(0 \lt 2(0) + 1\) is true.
  • Step 3: Shade the region below the line.

This shaded region represents all the \((x, y)\) pairs that make the inequality true.

Your answer
slides.study_tip: Watch the method, then close the solution and redo the problem from scratch.
slides.strategy_hint: Mark known angles and side relationships first, then use triangle or parallel-line facts.

slides.title: Up next System of Linear Inequalities
slides.html:
03Up next

System of Linear Inequalities

slides.title: System of Linear Inequalities
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

A system of linear inequalities is a set of two or more linear inequalities with the same variables.

Solution: The solution is the region where the shaded areas of all inequalities overlap.

Example:

\[ \begin{cases} y \le 2x + 3 \\ y > -x + 1 \end{cases} \]
  • Step 1: Graph each inequality on the same coordinate plane.
  • Step 2: Use dashed or solid lines depending on the symbols \(\lt \) or \(\le\), or \(>\) or \(\ge\).
  • Step 3: Shade the region for each inequality.
  • Step 4: The overlapping shaded region is the solution set.
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Question: System of Inequalities
slides.html:
Strategy

Plug the point into each inequality separately; both must be true.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

Given the system of inequalities:

\[ \begin{aligned} y &\leq x + 7 \\ y &\geq -2x - 1 \end{aligned} \]

Which point \((x, y)\) is a solution to the system?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: For a system, test each point in every inequality — only the overlap region counts.
slides.strategy_hint: Plug the point into each inequality separately; both must be true.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: System of Inequalities
slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Compare your work with the final answer and fix any missed step.

Check each point to see which satisfies both inequalities:

\[ y \leq x + 7 \text{and} y \geq -2x - 1 \]
  • (A) \(x = -14,\ y = 0\):
    \[ 0 \leq -14 + 7 = -7 \text{(False)} ⇒ \text{Reject} \]
  • (B) \(x = 0,\ y = -14\):
    \[ -14 \leq 0 + 7 = 7 \text{(True)}, -14 \geq -2(0) - 1 = -1 \text{(False)} ⇒ \text{Reject} \]
  • (C) \(x = 0,\ y = 14\):
    \[ 14 \leq 0 + 7 = 7 \text{(False)} ⇒ \text{Reject} \]
  • (D) \(x = 14,\ y = 0\):
    \[ 0 \leq 14 + 7 = 21 \text{(True)}, 0 \geq -2(14) - 1 = -29 \text{(True)} ⇒ \text{Accept} \]
Correct Answer
\(\boxed{D}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question: Inequality Table Matching
slides.html:
Strategy

Compute the boundary 6x + 2 at each x, then confirm every y is strictly below it.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

Given the inequality:

\[y \lt 6x + 2\]

For which of the following tables are all \((x, y)\) values solutions to the inequality?

A.

xy
320
532
744

B.

xy
316
536
740

C.

xy
316
528
740

D.

xy
324
536
748

Your answer
slides.study_tip: Substitute each table row into the inequality — all three rows must satisfy it.
slides.strategy_hint: Compute the boundary 6x + 2 at each x, then confirm every y is strictly below it.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Inequality Table Matching
slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Compare your work with the final answer and fix any missed step.

The inequality is:

\[y \lt 6x + 2\]
  • For \(x = 3\), \(6x + 2 = 20\)
  • For \(x = 5\), \(6x + 2 = 32\)
  • For \(x = 7\), \(6x + 2 = 44\)

Compare each \(y\) with the threshold values:

  • A: \(y = 20, 32, 44\) — not strictly less than the thresholds
  • B: \(y = 16 \lt 20\), but \(36 > 32\)
  • C: \(y = 16 \lt 20\), \(28 \lt 32\), \(40 \lt 44\)
  • D: \(y = 24 > 20\)
Correct Answer
\(\boxed{C}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Up next Real World Application
slides.html:
04Up next

Real World Application

slides.title: Real-World Application
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

Problem: A theater has a maximum capacity of 200 people. Tickets cost $10 for adults and $5 for students.

Write an inequality that shows all possible combinations of adults (\(a\)) and students (\(s\)) that can attend.

\[ 10a + 5s \le 200 \]

This shows combinations that keep the total cost at or under $200.
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Question: Shipment Inequality Problem
slides.html:
Strategy

Plug the point into each inequality separately; both must be true.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

A laundry service is buying detergent and fabric softener from its supplier. The supplier will deliver no more than 300 pounds in a shipment. Each container of detergent weighs 7.35 pounds, and each container of fabric softener weighs 6.2 pounds. The service wants to buy at least twice as many containers of detergent as containers of fabric softener.

Let \(d\) represent the number of detergent containers and \(s\) the number of softener containers. Which system of inequalities best represents this situation?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: For a system, test each point in every inequality — only the overlap region counts.
slides.strategy_hint: Plug the point into each inequality separately; both must be true.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Shipment Inequality Problem
slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Compare your work with the final answer and fix any missed step.

  • The total weight inequality is:
    \[ 7.35d + 6.2s \leq 300 \]
  • The service wants at least twice as many detergent containers as softener containers:
    \[ d \geq 2s \]
  • So the correct system is:
    \[ \begin{cases} 7.35d + 6.2s \leq 300 \\ d \geq 2s \end{cases} \]
Correct Answer
\(\boxed{A}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question: Psychologist Experiment — Inequality Problem
slides.html:
Strategy

Label opposite, adjacent, and hypotenuse relative to the given angle.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

A psychologist conducted an experiment with 300 people, each choosing the most appealing image from a set of five arranged in random order.

  • Of the first 150 participants, 36 chose the first picture.
  • Among the remaining 150 participants, \(p\) chose the first picture.
  • If more than 20% of all participants chose the first picture, which inequality best describes the possible values of \(p\)?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: When you multiply or divide by a negative, flip the inequality sign — then check with a test value.
slides.strategy_hint: Label opposite, adjacent, and hypotenuse relative to the given angle.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Psychologist Experiment — Inequality Problem
slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Compare your work with the final answer and fix any missed step.

Walk through the solution

Step 1: Total number of participants \(= 300\)

Step 2: Total number who chose the first picture \(= 36 + p\)

Step 3: More than 20% chose the first picture:

\[ 36 + p > 0.20 \times 300 ⇒ p + 36 > 60 ⇒ p > 24 \]
Correct Answer
\(\boxed{D}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question: Triangle Inequality Theorem
slides.html:
Strategy

Use a + b > c, a + c > b, and b + c > a — combine to get the valid range for the unknown side.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

The triangle inequality theorem states that the sum of any two sides of a triangle must be greater than the length of the third side.

If a triangle has side lengths of 6 and 12, which inequality represents the possible lengths, \(x\), of the third side of the triangle?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Triangle inequality: the third side must be greater than the difference and less than the sum of the other two sides.
slides.strategy_hint: Use a + b > c, a + c > b, and b + c > a — combine to get the valid range for the unknown side.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Triangle Inequality Theorem
slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Compare your work with the final answer and fix any missed step.

Triangle Inequality Theorem: The sum of the lengths of any two sides must be greater than the third side.

Let the three sides be: \(6\), \(12\), and \(x\).

Apply the triangle inequality:

\[\begin{aligned} 6 + 12 &> x ⇒ x \lt 18 \\ 6 + x &> 12 ⇒ x > 6 \\ 12 + x &> 6 ⇒ \text{Always true since } x > 0 \end{aligned}\]

Final range: \(6 \lt x \lt 18\)

Correct Answer
\(\boxed{C}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question: Graphing Inequality — Point Testing
slides.html:
Strategy

Boundary first: rewrite as an equation, then pick a test point to decide which side to shade.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

Given the inequality:

\[ y > 7x - 19 \]

Which of the following points in the \(xy\)-plane satisfies the given inequality?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Sketch or test regions; note solid vs dashed boundaries and whether endpoints are included.
slides.strategy_hint: Boundary first: rewrite as an equation, then pick a test point to decide which side to shade.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Graphing Inequality — Point Testing
slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Compare your work with the final answer and fix any missed step.

Step: Plug each point into the inequality \(y > 7x - 19\).

  • A. \(-32 > 7(-1) - 19 ⇒ -32 > -26\) — False
  • B. \(-20 > 7(0) - 19 ⇒ -20 > -19\) — False
  • C. \(-3 > 7(2) - 19 ⇒ -3 > -5\) — True
  • D. \(-14 > 7(4) - 19 ⇒ -14 > 9\) — False
Correct Answer
\(\boxed{C}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question: System of Inequalities — Graphical Solution
slides.html:
Strategy

Plug the point into each inequality separately; both must be true.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

Consider the following system of inequalities:

\[\begin{aligned} y &\lt -4x - 1 \\ y &> x + 2 \end{aligned}\]

Which point \((x, y)\) is a solution to this system in the \(xy\)-plane?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: For a system, test each point in every inequality — only the overlap region counts.
slides.strategy_hint: Plug the point into each inequality separately; both must be true.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: System of Inequalities — Graphical Solution
slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Compare your work with the final answer and fix any missed step.

Test each point in both inequalities:

A. \((1, 4)\):

\[y \lt -4x - 1 ⇒ 4 \lt -4(1) - 1 = -5 \text{False}\]

B. \((-1, 1)\):

\[y \lt -4x - 1 ⇒ 1 \lt 3 \text{True}, y > x + 2 ⇒ 1 > 1 \text{False}\]

C. \((-2, 2)\):

\[y \lt -4x - 1 ⇒ 2 \lt 7 \text{True}, y > x + 2 ⇒ 2 > 0 \text{True}\]

D. \((-3, -3)\):

\[y \lt -4x - 1 ⇒ -3 \lt 11 \text{True}, y > x + 2 ⇒ -3 > -1 \text{False}\]
Correct Answer
\(\boxed{C}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question: Inequality — Calories Burned by Jogging
slides.html:
Strategy

Boundary first: rewrite as an equation, then pick a test point to decide which side to shade.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

Melanie burns between 120 and 135 calories for each mile that she jogs.

Which inequality represents the total number of calories, \(y\),

Melanie could burn from jogging 14 miles?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: When you multiply or divide by a negative, flip the inequality sign — then check with a test value.
slides.strategy_hint: Boundary first: rewrite as an equation, then pick a test point to decide which side to shade.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Inequality — Calories Burned by Jogging
slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Compare your work with the final answer and fix any missed step.

Melanie burns 120 to 135 calories per mile.

Let \(y\) be the total calories burned in 14 miles.

Then:

\[ 14 \times 120 \leq y \leq 14 \times 135 \]

This simplifies to:

\[ 120 \leq \frac{y}{14} \leq 135 \]
Correct Answer
\(\boxed{D}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question: Challenging System of Inequalities
slides.html:
Strategy

Plug the point into each inequality separately; both must be true.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

Consider the system of inequalities below:

\[ \begin{aligned} y &\leq \frac{1}{2}x + 3 \\ y &> -2x + 1 \end{aligned} \]

Which of the following points lies within the solution region of this system?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: For a system, test each point in every inequality — only the overlap region counts.
slides.strategy_hint: Plug the point into each inequality separately; both must be true.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Challenging System of Inequalities
slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Compare your work with the final answer and fix any missed step.

Inequality 1: \(y \leq \frac{1}{2}x + 3\)

Inequality 2: \(y > -2x + 1\)

Test option D: \((0, 2)\)

\[ 2 \leq \frac{1}{2}(0) + 3 \text{True} \]
\[ 2 > -2(0) + 1 ⇒ 2 > 1 \text{True} \]
Correct Answer
\(\boxed{D}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Up next Exponent Expression
slides.html:
01Up next

Exponent Expression

slides.title: Definition of Exponents
slides.html:
Knowledge PointLearn the rule

Understand the definition, formula, or theorem before trying a problem.

Core concept

Understand this definition — every example and practice problem in this lesson builds on it.

Definition: An exponential expression has the form \(\displaystyle x^n\), where:

  • \(\displaystyle x\) is the base
  • \(\displaystyle n\) is the exponent or power

This represents the base \(\displaystyle x\) being multiplied by itself \(\displaystyle n\) times.

\[ x^n = \underbrace{x \cdot x \cdot x \cdots x}_{n \text{ times}} \]
slides.study_tip: Focus on definitions and vocabulary — SAT questions often test whether you recognize the form of an equation.

slides.title: Laws of Exponents (Part 1)
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

Key rules

Basic Properties

Identity\(\displaystyle x^1 = x\)e.g., \(\displaystyle 3^1 = 3\)
Zero Exponent\(\displaystyle x^0 = 1\)e.g., \(\displaystyle 3^0 = 1\)

Multiplication and Division Rules

Product Rule\(\displaystyle x^m \cdot x^n = x^{m+n}\)e.g., \(\displaystyle 3^4 \cdot 3^5 = 3^9\)
Quotient Rule\(\displaystyle \dfrac{x^m}{x^n} = x^{m-n}\)e.g., \(\displaystyle \dfrac{3^7}{3^3} = 3^4\)
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Laws of Exponents (Part 2)
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

Key rules

Power Rules

Power of a Power\(\displaystyle (x^m)^n = x^{mn}\)e.g., \(\displaystyle (3^2)^4 = 3^8\)
Power of a Product\(\displaystyle (xy)^m = x^m y^m\)e.g., \(\displaystyle (2 \cdot 3)^3 = 2^3 \cdot 3^3\)
Power of a Quotient\(\displaystyle \left(\dfrac{x}{y}\right)^m = \dfrac{x^m}{y^m}\)e.g., \(\displaystyle \left(\dfrac{2}{3}\right)^3 = \dfrac{2^3}{3^3}\)

Negative Exponent

Rule\(\displaystyle x^{-m} = \dfrac{1}{x^m}\)e.g., \(\displaystyle 3^{-4} = \dfrac{1}{3^4}\)
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Question: Product of Exponential Expressions
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify the initial value, growth or decay factor, and how the exponent scales with time.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

Which expression represents the product of

\[ (b^6 c^{-2} d^{-5}) \text{and} (b^8 c^{-3}+c^4 d^5), \]

where \( b, c, \) and \( d \) are positive?

Options:

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify the initial value, growth or decay factor, and how the exponent scales with time.

slides.title: Answer and Explanation
slides.html:
Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

Explanation:

We are asked to find the product of:

\[ (b^6 c^{-2} d^{-5}) \text{and} (b^8 c^{-3} + c^4 d^5) \]

Distribute the first term across both terms in the second expression:

\[ b^6 c^{-2} d^{-5} \cdot b^8 c^{-3} = b^{6+8} c^{-2-3} d^{-5} = b^{14} c^{-5} d^{-5} \]
\[ b^6 c^{-2} d^{-5} \cdot c^4 d^5 = b^6 c^{2} d^{0} = b^6 c^2 \]

Putting it together:

\[ b^{14} c^{-5} d^{-5} + b^6 c^2 \]

Now check the answer choices. Only choice C has this exact expression.

Final Answer
C \(b^{14} c^{-5} d^{-5} + b^6 c^2\)
slides.study_tip: For multiple choice, eliminate obviously wrong options before doing full algebra.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify the initial value, growth or decay factor, and how the exponent scales with time.

slides.title: Up next Radical & Roots
slides.html:
02Up next

Radical & Roots

slides.title: Radicals and Roots
slides.html:
Knowledge PointLearn the rule

Understand the definition, formula, or theorem before trying a problem.

Core concept

Understand this definition — every example and practice problem in this lesson builds on it.

Definition: A radical is the inverse of an exponent.

\[ \sqrt[n]{x} = x^{\frac{1}{n}} \]
  • \(\displaystyle \sqrt{x} = x^{\frac{1}{2}}\) (square root)
  • \(\displaystyle \sqrt[3]{x} = x^{\frac{1}{3}}\) (cube root)
  • \(\displaystyle \sqrt[4]{x^3} = x^{\frac{3}{4}}\) (fractional exponent)
slides.study_tip: Focus on definitions and vocabulary — SAT questions often test whether you recognize the form of an equation.

slides.title: Properties of Radicals
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

Properties
Property\(\displaystyle \sqrt{a} \cdot \sqrt{b} = \sqrt{ab}\)
Property\(\displaystyle \dfrac{\sqrt{a}}{\sqrt{b}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{a}{b}}\)
Property\(\displaystyle \sqrt[n]{x^m} = x^{\frac{m}{n}}\)

Important: All radicals can be rewritten using fractional exponents!
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Rationalizing Denominators
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

Goal: Remove square roots from denominators.

\[ \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} ⇒ \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \]

Use the conjugate if the denominator is a binomial:

\[ \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2} + 1} \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{2} - 1}{\sqrt{2} - 1} = \dfrac{\sqrt{2} - 1}{(\sqrt{2})^2 - 1^2} = \dfrac{\sqrt{2} - 1}{1} \]
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Question
slides.html:
Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

Simplify the expression:

\[ \left( \dfrac{27x^6 y^3}{81x^2 y^9} \right)^{\frac{1}{3}} \]
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify the initial value, growth or decay factor, and how the exponent scales with time.

slides.title: Solution
slides.html:
Solution ReviewStudy the reasoning

Read the steps carefully, then summarize the method in your own words.

✦
Your turnWork it out on paper first — reveal when you're ready.

Solution:

\[ = \left( \dfrac{27}{81} \cdot x^{6-2} \cdot y^{3-9} \right)^{\frac{1}{3}} = \left( \dfrac{1}{3} \cdot x^4 \cdot y^{-6} \right)^{\frac{1}{3}} = \dfrac{x^{\frac{4}{3}}}{3 \cdot y^2} \]
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question: Rational Exponents and Roots
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify the initial value, growth or decay factor, and how the exponent scales with time.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

If

\[ \frac{\sqrt{x^5}}{\sqrt[3]{x^4}} = x^{\frac{a}{b}} \]

for all positive values of \( x \), what is the value of \( \frac{a}{b} \)?

Your answer

Enter an exact fraction or decimal equivalent.
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify the initial value, growth or decay factor, and how the exponent scales with time.

slides.title: Solution
slides.html:
Solution ReviewStudy the reasoning

Read the steps carefully, then summarize the method in your own words.

✦
Your turnWork it out on paper first — reveal when you're ready.

Solution:

We use the property of radicals and exponents:

\[ \sqrt{x^5} = x^{5/2}, \sqrt[3]{x^4} = x^{4/3} \]
\[ \frac{x^{5/2}}{x^{4/3}} = x^{5/2 - 4/3} \]

Find a common denominator:

\[ \frac{5}{2} = \frac{15}{6}, \frac{4}{3} = \frac{8}{6} \]
\[ x^{15/6 - 8/6} = x^{7/6} \]

Thus,

\[ \frac{a}{b} = \frac{7}{6} \]
Answer
\(\boxed{\frac{7}{6}}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question: Exponent and Radical Expression Equivalence
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify the initial value, growth or decay factor, and how the exponent scales with time.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

For what value of \( x \) is the given expression equivalent to \( (70n)^{30x} \), where \( n > 1 \)?

\[ \sqrt[5]{70n \left( \sqrt[6]{70n} \right)^2} \]

Your answer

Enter an exact fraction or decimal equivalent.
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify the initial value, growth or decay factor, and how the exponent scales with time.

slides.title: Solution: Simplifying the Expression
slides.html:
Solution ReviewStudy the reasoning

Read the steps carefully, then summarize the method in your own words.

We begin with:

\[ \sqrt[5]{70n \left( \sqrt[6]{70n} \right)^2} = \sqrt[5]{70n \cdot (70n)^{2/6}} = \sqrt[5]{(70n)^{1 + \frac{2}{6}}} = \sqrt[5]{(70n)^{\frac{4}{3}}} \]

Now apply the outer root:

\[ \left( (70n)^{\frac{4}{3}} \right)^{1/5} = (70n)^{\frac{4}{15}} \]

We are told this is equal to:

\[ (70n)^{30x} ⇒ 30x = \frac{4}{15} ⇒ x = \frac{4}{450} = \frac{2}{225} \]
Answer
\(\boxed{\frac{2}{225}}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question
slides.html:
Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

If \(\displaystyle \left(5^3\right)^{4k} = \left(5^{\frac{1}{3}}\right)^{24}\), what is the value of \(\displaystyle k\)?
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify the initial value, growth or decay factor, and how the exponent scales with time.

slides.title: Answer
slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Compare your work with the final answer and fix any missed step.

We simplify both sides:

\[ (5^3)^{4k} = 5^{12k}, \left(5^{\frac{1}{3}}\right)^{24} = 5^{\frac{24}{3}} = 5^8 \]

So,

\[ 5^{12k} = 5^8 ⇒ 12k = 8 ⇒ k = \frac{2}{3} \]
Correct answer
B
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question
slides.html:
Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

Which expression is equivalent to \(\displaystyle 4^{m+2}\)?

A) \(\displaystyle 16^m\)

B) \(\displaystyle 16 + 4^m\)

C) \(\displaystyle 8(4^m)\)

D) \(\displaystyle 16(4^m)\)
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify the initial value, growth or decay factor, and how the exponent scales with time.

slides.title: Answer
slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Compare your work with the final answer and fix any missed step.

We simplify the original expression:

\[ 4^{m+2} = 4^m \cdot 4^2 = 4^m \cdot 16 = 16(4^m) \]
Correct answer
D
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify the initial value, growth or decay factor, and how the exponent scales with time.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

\[ \sqrt[8]{41k} \left( \sqrt[9]{41k} \right)^{12} \]

For what value of \(\displaystyle x\) is the given expression equivalent to \(\displaystyle (41k)^{25x}\), where \(\displaystyle k > 1\)?

Your answer

Enter an exact fraction or decimal equivalent.
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify the initial value, growth or decay factor, and how the exponent scales with time.

slides.title: Answer
slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Compare your work with the final answer and fix any missed step.

Correct answer
\(\displaystyle x = \dfrac{7}{120}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question
slides.html:
Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

\[ \frac{10 \sqrt[6]{9^3 \cdot x^{48}}}{\sqrt[4]{6^4 x}} \]

The given expression is equivalent to \(\displaystyle a x^b\), where \(\displaystyle a > 0\), \(\displaystyle b > 0\), and \(\displaystyle x > 1\). What is the value of \(\displaystyle ab\)?
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify the initial value, growth or decay factor, and how the exponent scales with time.

slides.title: Answer
slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Compare your work with the final answer and fix any missed step.

We simplify numerator and denominator:

Numerator:

\[ 10 \cdot \sqrt[6]{9^3 \cdot x^{48}} = 10 \cdot (9^3 \cdot x^{48})^{1/6} = 10 \cdot 9^{1/2} \cdot x^8 \]

Since \(\displaystyle 9 = 3^2\), so \(\displaystyle 9^{1/2} = 3\), hence numerator becomes:

\[ 10 \cdot 3 \cdot x^8 = 30x^8 \]

Denominator:

\[ \sqrt[4]{6^4 x} = (6^4 x)^{1/4} = 6 \cdot x^{1/4} \]

Entire expression:

\[ \frac{30x^8}{6x^{1/4}} = 5x^{8 - 1/4} = 5x^{\frac{31}{4}} \]

So \(\displaystyle a = 5\), \(\displaystyle b = \frac{31}{4}\)

\[ ab = 5 \cdot \frac{31}{4} = \frac{155}{4} \]
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question
slides.html:
Strategy

Label opposite, adjacent, and hypotenuse relative to the given angle.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

\[ \sqrt[4]{x^3} \cdot \sqrt[12]{x^5} \]

The given expression is equivalent to \(\displaystyle \sqrt[n]{x^m}\), where \(\displaystyle m\) and \(\displaystyle n\) are positive constants each less than 10 and \(\displaystyle x > 1\). What is the value of \(\displaystyle \dfrac{m}{n}\)?

Your answer

Enter an exact fraction or decimal equivalent.
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Label opposite, adjacent, and hypotenuse relative to the given angle.

slides.title: Answer
slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Compare your work with the final answer and fix any missed step.

We simplify the expression:

\[ \sqrt[4]{x^3} \cdot \sqrt[12]{x^5} = x^{\frac{3}{4}} \cdot x^{\frac{5}{12}} = x^{\frac{3}{4} + \frac{5}{12}} \]

To add the exponents:

\[ \frac{3}{4} = \frac{9}{12}, \frac{9}{12} + \frac{5}{12} = \frac{14}{12} = \frac{7}{6} \]

So the expression becomes:

\[ x^{\frac{7}{6}} = \sqrt[6]{x^7} \]

Therefore, \(\displaystyle m = 7\), \(\displaystyle n = 6\), and

\[ \frac{m}{n} = \frac{7}{6} \]
Correct answer
\(\displaystyle \dfrac{7}{6}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Up next Combine Like Terms and Simplify
slides.html:
01Up next

Combine Like Terms and Simplify

slides.title: Combining Like Terms
slides.html:
Knowledge PointLearn the rule

Understand the definition, formula, or theorem before trying a problem.

Core concept

Understand this definition — every example and practice problem in this lesson builds on it.

Definition: Combine terms with the exact same variables and powers.

Example:

\[ 2(2a^2 - 3a^2b^2 - 4b^2) - (a^2 + 5a^2b^2 - 10b^2) \]

Distribute:

\[ = 4a^2 - 6a^2b^2 - 8b^2 - a^2 - 5a^2b^2 + 10b^2 \]

Combine like terms:

\[ = 3a^2 - 11a^2b^2 + 2b^2 \]
slides.study_tip: Focus on definitions and vocabulary — SAT questions often test whether you recognize the form of an equation.

slides.title: Simplifying Fractional Expressions
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

Rules for Simplifying:

  • Look for common factors in numerator and denominator.
  • Apply factoring where possible.
  • Cancel shared factors.

Example:

\[ \frac{4xy(x+y)(x-y)}{6x(x-y)} = \frac{2y(x+y)}{3} \]

Note: Do not cancel terms unless they are factors!
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Question
slides.html:
Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

Which expression is equivalent to

\[ (3x^3 + 8x^2 - 4x) + (7x^2 - 11x - 7)\ ? \]

A) \(\displaystyle 3x^3 + x^2 - 15x - 7\)

B) \(\displaystyle 3x^3 + 15x^2 - 15x - 7\)

C) \(\displaystyle 10x^5 - 7x - 7\)

D) \(\displaystyle 15x^4 + 3x^3 - 15x^2 - 7\)
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer
slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Compare your work with the final answer and fix any missed step.

Combine like terms:

\[ (3x^3 + 8x^2 - 4x) + (7x^2 - 11x - 7) = 3x^3 + (8x^2 + 7x^2) + (-4x - 11x) - 7 = 3x^3 + 15x^2 - 15x - 7 \]
Correct answer
B
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Up next Expansion
slides.html:
02Up next

Expansion

slides.title: Expansion Using FOIL
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

FOIL Method is a technique used to expand the product of two binomials.

FOIL stands for:

  • F – First: Multiply the first terms in each binomial
  • O – Outer: Multiply the outer terms
  • I – Inner: Multiply the inner terms
  • L – Last: Multiply the last terms in each binomial
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Expansion Using FOIL
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

Example: Expand \(\displaystyle (x + 3)(x - 5)\)

\[ \begin{aligned} \text{First: } & x \cdot x = x^2 \\ \text{Outer: } & x \cdot (-5) = -5x \\ \text{Inner: } & 3 \cdot x = 3x \\ \text{Last: } & 3 \cdot (-5) = -15 \\ \end{aligned} \]

Combine like terms:

\[ x^2 - 5x + 3x - 15 = x^2 - 2x - 15 \]
Final Answer
\(\displaystyle x^2 - 2x - 15\)
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Question: Find the Value of ab
slides.html:
Strategy

Label opposite, adjacent, and hypotenuse relative to the given angle.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

The equation below is true for all \( x \), where \( a \) and \( b \) are constants:

\[ (ax + 3)(5x^2 - bx + 4) = 20x^3 - 9x^2 - 2x + 12 \]

What is the value of \( ab \)?

Options:

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Label opposite, adjacent, and hypotenuse relative to the given angle.

slides.title: Solution: Matching Coefficients
slides.html:
Solution ReviewStudy the reasoning

Read the steps carefully, then summarize the method in your own words.

We expand the left-hand side:

\[ (ax + 3)(5x^2 - bx + 4)= 5a x^3 - ab x^2 + 4a x + 15x^2 - 3b x + 12 \]

Now combine like terms:

\[ 5a x^3 + (-ab + 15)x^2 + (4a - 3b)x + 12 \]

Match with the right-hand side:

\[ 20x^3 - 9x^2 - 2x + 12 \]

Equating coefficients:

\[ 5a = 20 ⇒ a = 4 \]
\[ -ab + 15 = -9 ⇒ -ab = -24 ⇒ ab = 24 \]
Answer
\(\boxed{24}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question
slides.html:
Strategy

Label opposite, adjacent, and hypotenuse relative to the given angle.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

The expression \(\displaystyle 5x^5 + 6x^4 - 8x^3\) can be rewritten as

\[ (x^3 - hx^2)(5x^2 + 10x), \]

where \(\displaystyle h\) is a constant. What is the value of \(\displaystyle h\)?

Your answer

Enter an exact fraction or decimal equivalent.
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Label opposite, adjacent, and hypotenuse relative to the given angle.

slides.title: Answer
slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Compare your work with the final answer and fix any missed step.

We are given:

\[ (x^3 - hx^2)(5x^2 + 10x) \]

Use distributive property:

\[ x^3(5x^2 + 10x) - hx^2(5x^2 + 10x) = 5x^5 + 10x^4 - 5h x^4 - 10h x^3 \]

Combine like terms:

\[ 5x^5 + (10 - 5h)x^4 - 10h x^3 \]

Compare with the original expression:

\[ 5x^5 + 6x^4 - 8x^3 \]

Match coefficients:

\[ 10 - 5h = 6 ⇒ 5h = 4 ⇒ h = \frac{4}{5} \]
Correct answer
\(\displaystyle h = \dfrac{4}{5}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Up next Factoring
slides.html:
03Up next

Factoring

slides.title: Factoring Basics
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

Factoring is the process of expressing a polynomial as a product of simpler expressions.

Step 1: Factor out the Greatest Common Factor (GCF)

\[ 6x^2 + 9x = 3x(2x + 3) \]

Step 2: Factoring Trinomials when \(\displaystyle a = 1\)

Method: Find two numbers that:

  • Multiply to \(\displaystyle c\)
  • Add to \(\displaystyle b\)

Example:

\[ x^2 + 5x + 6 = (x + 2)(x + 3) \]

Tip: Always factor out the GCF before factoring the trinomial.
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Factoring When $a 1$ (Cross Method)
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

When the leading coefficient \(\displaystyle a \ne 1\), we use the trial-and-error or Chinese Cross Method :

Example:

\[ 6x^2 + 7x + 2 ⇒ (3x + 2)(2x + 1) \]

Reasoning:

  • \(\displaystyle 3x \cdot 1 = 3x\)
  • \(\displaystyle 2x \cdot 2 = 4x\)
  • \(\displaystyle 3x + 4x = 7x\)

General Tip:

  • Look for two pairs of factors of \(\displaystyle a\) and \(\displaystyle c\) whose cross-products add to \(\displaystyle b\).
  • Always check your result by expanding (FOIL method).
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Question: Factoring a Quadratic Expression
slides.html:
Strategy

Use the graph shape, vertex or symmetry, and discriminant to narrow the answer.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

Which of the following expressions is(are) a factor of

\[ 3x^2 + 20x - 63? \]
  • I. \(x - 9\)
  • II. \(3x - 7\)

Options:

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: For multiple choice, eliminate obviously wrong options before doing full algebra.
slides.strategy_hint: Use the graph shape, vertex or symmetry, and discriminant to narrow the answer.

slides.title: Solution: Use Factoring or Substitution
slides.html:
Solution ReviewStudy the reasoning

Read the steps carefully, then summarize the method in your own words.

We are given the quadratic expression:

\[ 3x^2 + 20x - 63 \]

Let's factor it:

\[ 3x^2 + 20x - 63 = (x - 3)(3x + 21) \]

But:

\[ (x - 9)(3x - 7) = 3x^2 - 7x - 27x + 63 = 3x^2 - 34x + 63 \neq 3x^2 + 20x - 63 \]

Let’s check factoring by grouping:

\[ 3x^2 + 27x - 7x - 63 = 3x(x + 9) -7(x + 9) = (x + 9)(3x - 7) \]

So the factors are:

\[ (x + 9)(3x - 7) \]

Correct Factor: \(3x - 7\)

Answer
\(\boxed{B. II only}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question: Finding the Smallest Possible Factor Constant
slides.html:
Strategy

Label opposite, adjacent, and hypotenuse relative to the given angle.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

One of the factors of

\[ 2x^3 + 42x^2 + 208x \]

is \(x + b\), where \(b\) is a positive constant.

What is the smallest possible value of \(b\)?

Your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Label opposite, adjacent, and hypotenuse relative to the given angle.

slides.title: Solution: Factor by Grouping and Cross Multiplication
slides.html:
Solution ReviewStudy the reasoning

Read the steps carefully, then summarize the method in your own words.

We are given:

\[ 2x^3 + 42x^2 + 208x \]

First, factor out the GCF:

\[ 2x(x^2 + 21x + 104) \]

Now, factor the quadratic using cross multiplication (a.k.a. the AC method). We're looking for two numbers that multiply to:

\[ 1 \cdot 104 = 104 \text{and add up to } 21. \]

The pair is \(13\) and \(8\), since:

\[ 13 \cdot 8 = 104 \text{and} 13 + 8 = 21. \]

So we factor the quadratic:

\[ x^2 + 21x + 104 = (x + 13)(x + 8) \]

Therefore, the full factorization is:

\[ 2x(x + 13)(x + 8) \]

Since the question asks for the smallest possible positive value of \(b\) such that \(x + b\) is a factor, the answer is:

\[ \boxed{8} \]
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question: Factoring with Decimal Coefficients
slides.html:
Strategy

Label opposite, adjacent, and hypotenuse relative to the given angle.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

The expression

\[ 0.36x^2 + 0.63x + 1.17 \]

can be rewritten as

\[ a(4x^2 + 7x + 13), \]

where \(\displaystyle a\) is a constant. What is the value of \(\displaystyle a\)?

Your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Label opposite, adjacent, and hypotenuse relative to the given angle.

slides.title: Answer and Explanation
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

We are given:

\[ 0.36x^2 + 0.63x + 1.17 = a(4x^2 + 7x + 13) \]

Match the coefficients:

\[ a \cdot 4 = 0.36 ⇒ a = \frac{0.36}{4} = 0.09 \]
Correct Answer
\(a =0.09\)
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Factoring Rational Expressions (Part 1)
slides.html:
Knowledge PointLearn the rule

Understand the definition, formula, or theorem before trying a problem.

Core concept

Understand this definition — every example and practice problem in this lesson builds on it.

Definition: A rational expression is a fraction where both the numerator and the denominator are polynomials.

Goal: Simplify by factoring both the numerator and denominator, then cancelling common factors.

Steps to Simplify:

  1. Factor the numerator and denominator completely.
  2. Identify common factors in both.
  3. Cancel out the common factors.
  4. State restrictions: Any value that makes the denominator zero must be excluded from the domain.

Note: Only factors (not terms) can be cancelled.

\[ \frac{x^2 - 9}{x^2 - x - 6} = \frac{(x - 3)(x + 3)}{(x - 3)(x + 2)} = \frac{x + 3}{x + 2} \text{with } x \ne 3, -2 \]
slides.study_tip: Focus on definitions and vocabulary — SAT questions often test whether you recognize the form of an equation.

slides.title: Factoring Rational Expressions (Part 2)
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

Special Factoring Patterns to Know:

  • Difference of squares: \(\displaystyle a^2 - b^2 = (a - b)(a + b)\)
  • Perfect square trinomials: \(\displaystyle a^2 \pm 2ab + b^2 = (a \pm b)^2\)
  • Difference and sum of cubes:
    \[ a^3 - b^3 = (a - b)(a^2 + ab + b^2) \]
    \[ a^3 + b^3 = (a + b)(a^2 - ab + b^2) \]

Example:

\[ \frac{4x^2 - 9}{2x + 3} = \frac{(2x - 3)(2x + 3)}{2x + 3} = 2x - 3 \text{with } x \ne -\frac{3}{2} \]

Tip:

  • Always check if the polynomial is factorable before attempting to cancel.
  • Watch for common mistakes like cancelling terms instead of factors.
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Question: Polynomial Division
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

Given:

\[ \frac{x^2 + 6x - 7}{x + 7} = ax + d \]

The equation is true for all \( x \ne -7 \), where \( a \) and \( d \) are integers.

What is the value of \( a + d \)?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer and Explanation
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

We are dividing a quadratic by a linear binomial:

\[ \frac{x^2 + 6x - 7}{x + 7} \]

We use polynomial division or factoring. First, factor the numerator:

\[ x^2 + 6x - 7 = (x + 7)(x - 1) \]

So:

\[ \frac{(x + 7)(x - 1)}{x + 7} = x - 1 \text{for } x \ne -7 \]

Thus,

\[ ax + d = x - 1 ⇒ a = 1, d = -1 \]

Therefore,

\[ a + d = 1 + (-1) = \boxed{0} \]
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Question: Simplifying Rational Expressions
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

Let:

\[ f(x) = x^3 - 9x, g(x) = x^2 - 2x - 3 \]

Which of the following expressions is equivalent to:

\[ \frac{f(x)}{g(x)} \text{for } x > 3 \]

Options:

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: For multiple choice, eliminate obviously wrong options before doing full algebra.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer and Explanation
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

We start by factoring both the numerator and denominator:

\[ f(x) = x^3 - 9x = x(x^2 - 9) = x(x - 3)(x + 3) \]
\[ g(x) = x^2 - 2x - 3 = (x - 3)(x + 1) \]

Now simplify:

\[ \frac{f(x)}{g(x)} = \frac{x(x - 3)(x + 3)}{(x - 3)(x + 1)} = \frac{x(x + 3)}{x + 1} \]

So the correct answer is:

\[ \boxed{\text{D. } \frac{x(x + 3)}{x + 1}} \]
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Up next Fraction
slides.html:
04Up next

Fraction

slides.title: Adding Algebraic Fractions
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

To add or subtract algebraic fractions:

  • Step 1: Find a common denominator.
  • Step 2: Rewrite each fraction with the common denominator.
  • Step 3: Combine the numerators.
  • Step 4: Simplify if possible.

Example:

\[ \frac{1}{x} + \frac{1}{x+1} \]

Step 1: Common denominator is \(\displaystyle x(x+1)\)

\[ \frac{1}{x} = \frac{x+1}{x(x+1)}, \frac{1}{x+1} = \frac{x}{x(x+1)} \]

Step 2: Add the numerators:

\[ \frac{x+1}{x(x+1)} + \frac{x}{x(x+1)} = \frac{(x+1) + x}{x(x+1)} = \frac{2x+1}{x(x+1)} \]

Tip: Always check if you can factor or simplify the final expression.
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Flipping and Splitting Fractions
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

1. Flipping Fractions (Reciprocals):

Rule: When dividing by a fraction, multiply by its reciprocal.

\[ \frac{a}{b} \div \frac{c}{d} = \frac{a}{b} \cdot \frac{d}{c} \]

Example:

\[ \frac{3}{4} \div \frac{2}{5} = \frac{3}{4} \cdot \frac{5}{2} = \frac{15}{8} \]

Tip: Always flip the second fraction and multiply.

2. Splitting Fractions:

Rule: A fraction with addition or subtraction in the numerator can be split into two separate fractions:

\[ \frac{a + b}{c} = \frac{a}{c} + \frac{b}{c}, \frac{a - b}{c} = \frac{a}{c} - \frac{b}{c} \]

But: You cannot split a denominator:

\[ \frac{a}{b + c} \ne \frac{a}{b} + \frac{a}{c} \]

Example:

\[ \frac{2x + 6}{x} = \frac{2x}{x} + \frac{6}{x} = 2 + \frac{6}{x} \]

Tip: Only split the numerator when the denominator is a single term.
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Question:Rational Expressions
slides.html:
Strategy

Label opposite, adjacent, and hypotenuse relative to the given angle.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

Question:

\[ \frac{2}{x - 2} + \frac{3}{x + 5} = \frac{rx + t}{(x - 2)(x + 5)} \]

This equation is true for all \(\displaystyle x > 2\), where \(\displaystyle r\) and \(\displaystyle t\) are positive constants.

What is the value of \(\displaystyle rt\)?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Label opposite, adjacent, and hypotenuse relative to the given angle.

slides.title: Solution: Rational Expressions
slides.html:
Solution ReviewStudy the reasoning

Read the steps carefully, then summarize the method in your own words.

Start by finding a common denominator:

\[ \frac{2}{x - 2} + \frac{3}{x + 5} = \frac{2(x + 5) + 3(x - 2)}{(x - 2)(x + 5)} \]

Now expand the numerator:

\[ 2(x + 5) + 3(x - 2) = 2x + 10 + 3x - 6 = 5x + 4 \]

So the left-hand side becomes:

\[ \frac{5x + 4}{(x - 2)(x + 5)} \]

Compare with the right-hand side:

\[ \frac{rx + t}{(x - 2)(x + 5)} ⇒ rx + t = 5x + 4 \]

So:

\[ r = 5, t = 4 ⇒ rt = 5 \cdot 4 = \boxed{20} \]

Answer: (C) 20
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Up next Isolate a Variable
slides.html:
05Up next

Isolate a Variable

slides.title: Cross-Multiplication Technique
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

When you have an equation of the form:

\[ \frac{A}{B} = \frac{C}{D} \]

You can use cross-multiplication to eliminate denominators:

\[ A \cdot D = B \cdot C \]

Example:

\[ \frac{u - 3}{1} = \frac{6}{t - 2} ⇒ (u - 3)(t - 2) = 6 \]

Why it works: This is based on the property of proportions. Multiplying both sides by the least common denominator (LCD) clears all denominators in one step.
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Clearing Square Roots: Squaring Both Sides
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

If a variable is under a square root, square both sides to eliminate the radical.

General Rule:

\[ \sqrt{E} = F ⇒ E = F^2 \]

Example:

\[ 2\sqrt{w + 19} = \frac{14x}{7y} ⇒ \sqrt{w + 19} = \frac{2x}{y} ⇒ w + 19 = \left( \frac{2x}{y} \right)^2 \]

Tip: Always isolate the radical before squaring both sides.
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Solving Literal Equations (Isolate a Variable)
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

Literal equations are formulas or equations involving multiple variables. The goal is to solve for one variable in terms of the others.

Key Steps:

  • Step 1: Identify the variable you want to isolate (make it the subject).
  • Step 2: Use inverse operations (addition, subtraction, multiplication, division) to isolate that variable.
  • Step 3: If needed, eliminate radicals or simplify fractions.

Tips:

  • Use cross-multiplication when variables appear in denominators.
  • Clear square roots by squaring both sides of the equation.
  • Watch for extraneous solutions if squaring or multiplying both sides.
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Question: Solving for $w$ in terms of $x$ and $y$
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify the initial value, growth or decay factor, and how the exponent scales with time.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

Given the equation:

\[ \frac{14x}{7y} = 2\sqrt{w + 19} \]

The equation relates the distinct positive real numbers \(\displaystyle w\), \(\displaystyle x\), and \(\displaystyle y\).

Which equation correctly expresses \(\displaystyle w\) in terms of \(\displaystyle x\) and \(\displaystyle y\)?

Options:

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify the initial value, growth or decay factor, and how the exponent scales with time.

slides.title: Answer and Explanation
slides.html:
Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

Start with the equation:

\[ \frac{14x}{7y} = 2\sqrt{w + 19} ⇒ 2\sqrt{w + 19} = 2\sqrt{w + 19} ⇒ \frac{14x}{7y} = 2\sqrt{w + 19} ⇒ \frac{2x}{y} = \sqrt{w + 19} \]

Square both sides:

\[ \left(\frac{2x}{y}\right)^2 = w + 19 ⇒ \frac{4x^2}{y^2} = w + 19 ⇒ w = \frac{4x^2}{y^2} - 19 \]

Now compare this with the answer choices. Only option C correctly represents the structure:

\[ w = \left(\frac{x}{y}\right)^2 - 19 \]

Correct Answer: C
slides.study_tip: For multiple choice, eliminate obviously wrong options before doing full algebra.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify the initial value, growth or decay factor, and how the exponent scales with time.

slides.title: Question: Solving for $t$ in terms of $u$
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

Given the equation:

\[ u - 3 = \frac{6}{t - 2} \]

What is \(\displaystyle t\) in terms of \(\displaystyle u\)?

Options:

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer and Explanation
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

Starting from the equation:

\[ u - 3 = \frac{6}{t - 2} \]

Multiply both sides by \(\displaystyle (t - 2)\):

\[ (u - 3)(t - 2) = 6 \]

Expand the left-hand side:

\[ ut - 2u - 3t + 6 = 6 ⇒ ut - 3t = 2u ⇒ t(u - 3) = 2u ⇒ t = \frac{2u}{u - 3} \]

Correct Answer: D
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Question
slides.html:
Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

Which expression is equivalent to

\[ \frac{y - 3xy}{2x^2 - 2xy} + \frac{1 + 3y}{x - y}\ ? \]

A) \(\displaystyle \dfrac{2x - 3xy + 4y}{2x^2 - 2xy}\)

B) \(\displaystyle \dfrac{2x + 3xy + y}{2x^2 - 2xy}\)

C) \(\displaystyle \dfrac{2x + 3xy + y}{2x^3 - 4x^2y + 2xy^2}\)

D) \(\displaystyle \dfrac{4y - 3xy + 1}{2x^3 - 4x^2y + 2xy^2}\)
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer
slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Compare your work with the final answer and fix any missed step.

We simplify each term separately:

First term:

\[ \frac{y - 3xy}{2x^2 - 2xy} = \frac{y(1 - 3x)}{2x(x - y)} \]

Second term:

\[ \frac{1 + 3y}{x - y} \]

To combine these, we need a common denominator. The least common denominator (LCD) is \(\displaystyle 2x(x - y)\).

Rewrite the second term using LCD:

\[ \frac{1 + 3y}{x - y} = \frac{(1 + 3y) \cdot 2x}{2x(x - y)} = \frac{2x + 6xy}{2x(x - y)} \]

Now add the two terms:

\[ \frac{y - 3xy + 2x + 6xy}{2x^2 - 2xy} = \frac{2x + 3xy + y}{2x^2 - 2xy} \]
Correct answer
B
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question
slides.html:
Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

If \(\displaystyle \dfrac{4}{k + 2} = \dfrac{x}{3}\), where \(\displaystyle k \ne -2\), which equation correctly expresses \(\displaystyle k\) in terms of \(\displaystyle x\)?

A) \(\displaystyle k = \dfrac{12 - 2x}{x}\)

B) \(\displaystyle k = \dfrac{12 + 2x}{x}\)

C) \(\displaystyle k = \dfrac{x}{12 + 2x}\)

D) \(\displaystyle k = 12x - 2\)
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer
slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Compare your work with the final answer and fix any missed step.

We are given:

\[ \frac{4}{k + 2} = \frac{x}{3} \]

Cross-multiply:

\[ 4 \cdot 3 = x(k + 2) ⇒ 12 = xk + 2x \]

Solve for \(\displaystyle k\):

\[ 12 - 2x = xk ⇒ k = \frac{12 - 2x}{x} \]
Correct answer
A
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question
slides.html:
Strategy

Isolate the radical, square both sides if needed, then check for extraneous solutions.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

\[ T = 2P \sqrt{\frac{L}{G}} \]

The given equation relates the positive real numbers \(\displaystyle G\), \(\displaystyle L\), \(\displaystyle P\), and \(\displaystyle T\). Which equation correctly expresses \(\displaystyle G\) in terms of \(\displaystyle L\), \(\displaystyle P\), and \(\displaystyle T\)?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Isolate the radical, square both sides if needed, then check for extraneous solutions.

slides.title: Answer
slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Compare your work with the final answer and fix any missed step.

We start with the equation:

\[ T = 2P \sqrt{\frac{L}{G}} \]

Divide both sides by \(\displaystyle 2P\):

\[ \frac{T}{2P} = \sqrt{\frac{L}{G}} \]

Square both sides:

\[ \left( \frac{T}{2P} \right)^2 = \frac{L}{G} ⇒ \frac{T^2}{4P^2} = \frac{L}{G} \]

Solve for \(\displaystyle G\):

\[ G = \frac{4P^2 L}{T^2} \]
Correct answer
D
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question: Integer Relationship in Factored Form
slides.html:
Strategy

Label opposite, adjacent, and hypotenuse relative to the given angle.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

The expression

\[ 4x^2 + bx - 45 \]

where \(\displaystyle b\) is a constant, can be rewritten as

\[ (hx + k)(x + j), \]

where \(\displaystyle h\), \(\displaystyle k\), and \(\displaystyle j\) are 、integer constants. Which of the following must be an integer?

Options:

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: For multiple choice, eliminate obviously wrong options before doing full algebra.
slides.strategy_hint: Label opposite, adjacent, and hypotenuse relative to the given angle.

slides.title: Answer: Integer Relationship in Factored Form
slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Compare your work with the final answer and fix any missed step.

We are given:

\[ 4x^2 + bx - 45 = (hx + k)(x + j) \]

Expanding the right-hand side:

\[ hx^2 + (hj + k)x + kj \]

By comparing coefficients:

\[ h = 4, b = hj + k, kj = -45 \]

Since \(\displaystyle k\) and \(\displaystyle j\) are integers and their product \(\displaystyle kj = -45\), it follows that \(\displaystyle k\) must divide \(\displaystyle 45\). Therefore:

\[ \frac{45}{k} \text{ must be an integer}. \]
Correct Answer
D \(\frac{45}{k}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Parabolas
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

A parabola is a symmetric curve that satisfies a quadratic function. Below are different forms of its equation and key properties.
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Up next Different Forms of a Parabola
slides.html:
01Up next

Different Forms of a Parabola

slides.title: Standard Form
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

Standard Form:

\[ f(x) = ax^2 + bx + c \]

where:

  • \( a \) determines the direction and width of the parabola.
    • If \( a > 0 \), the parabola opens upward (concave up).
    • If \(a \lt 0\), the parabola opens downward (concave down).
  • \( b \) affects the location of the vertex along the \( x \)-axis.
  • \( c \) is the \( y \)-intercept.
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Vertex Form
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

Vertex Form:

\[ f(x) = a(x - h)^2 + k \]

where \( (h, k) \) is the vertex of the parabola.
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Factored Form
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

Factored Form:

\[ f(x) = a(x - m)(x - n) \]

where \( m \) and \( n \) are the \( x \)-intercepts (roots) of the parabola.
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Question: Quadratic Function from Three Conditions
slides.html:
Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

Problem:

A quadratic function \(\displaystyle f(x)\) satisfies the following:

  • The vertex of the parabola is at \(\displaystyle (3, -16)\).
  • The graph passes through the point \(\displaystyle (5, -12)\).
  • One of the roots of \(\displaystyle f(x)\) is \(\displaystyle 1\).

Tasks:

  1. Write the function in vertex form.
  2. Convert your function to standard form.
  3. Express the function in factored form.
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Label opposite, adjacent, and hypotenuse relative to the given angle.

slides.title: Solution: Challenge Problem
slides.html:
Solution ReviewStudy the reasoning

Read the steps carefully, then summarize the method in your own words.

Walk through the solution

Step 1: Vertex Form

\[ f(x) = a(x - 3)^2 - 16 \]

Plug in \(\displaystyle (5, -12)\) to solve for \(\displaystyle a\):

\[ -12 = a(5 - 3)^2 - 16 ⇒ -12 = 4a - 16 ⇒ 4a = 4 ⇒ a = 1 \]
\[ ⇒ f(x) = (x - 3)^2 - 16 \]

Step 2: Standard Form

\[ f(x) = x^2 - 6x + 9 - 16 = x^2 - 6x - 7 \]

Step 3: Factored Form

\[ f(x) = (x - 7)(x + 1) \]

(One root is \(\displaystyle 1\), so the other must be \(\displaystyle 7\) since sum of roots = \(\displaystyle 6\))
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Up next Properties of a Parabola
slides.html:
02Up next

Properties of a Parabola

slides.title: Vertex
slides.html:

\[ f(x) = ax^2 + bx + c \]

Vertex:

\[ x = \frac{-b}{2a}, y = f\left(\frac{-b}{2a}\right)=\frac{4ac-b^2}{4a} \]

slides.title: Question: Minimum Value of Transformed Quadratic Function
slides.html:
Strategy

Use the graph shape, vertex or symmetry, and discriminant to narrow the answer.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

The function \( f(x) = 4x^2 + 64x + 262 \).

The function \( g \) is defined by:

\[ g(x) = f(x + 5) \]

For what value of \( x \) does \( g(x) \) reach its minimum?

Options:

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Use the graph shape, vertex or symmetry, and discriminant to narrow the answer.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Minimum of g(x) = f(x+5)
slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Compare your work with the final answer and fix any missed step.

Walk through the solution

Correct Answer: \(\boxed{-13}\)

Step 1: Find the vertex of the quadratic function

Since \( f(x) = ax^2 + bx + c \), the vertex is at:

\[ x = -\frac{b}{2a} = -\frac{64}{2(4)} = -8 \]

So, the minimum of \( f(x) \) occurs at \( x = -8 \)

Step 2: Apply the transformation to \( g(x) = f(x+5) \)

This is a horizontal shift left by 5 units.

So the minimum of \( g(x) \) occurs at:

\[ x + 5 = -8 ⇒ x = -13 \]
Final Answer
\(\boxed{-13}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question: Minimum of Quadratic Function (Factored Form)
slides.html:
Strategy

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

The function is defined as:

\[ f(x) = (x - 14)(x + 19) \]

For what value of \( x \) does \( f(x) \) reach its minimum?

Options:

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Use the graph shape, vertex or symmetry, and discriminant to narrow the answer.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Minimum of f(x) = (x - 14)(x + 19)
slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Compare your work with the final answer and fix any missed step.

Walk through the solution

Correct Answer: \(\boxed{D}\)

Step 1: Use the axis of symmetry formula for a quadratic in factored form:

\[ x = \frac{r_1 + r_2}{2} \]

where \( r_1 = 14 \), \( r_2 = -19 \)

\[ x = \frac{14 + (-19)}{2} = \frac{-5}{2} = -\frac{5}{2} \]

slides.title: Question: Properties of a Quadratic Function
slides.html:
Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

The function is given as:

\[ f(x) = ax^2 + 4x + c \]

where \(a\) and \(c\) are constants. The parabola opens upward and it is also given that:

  • \(f(-9) = f(3)\)

What is the value of \(\displaystyle a\)?
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Label opposite, adjacent, and hypotenuse relative to the given angle.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Use of Symmetry and Vertex
slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Compare your work with the final answer and fix any missed step.

Walk through the solution

We are told:

\[ f(x) = ax^2 + 4x + c \text{and} f(-9) = f(3) \]

Step 1: Use symmetry of a parabola.

If \(f(-9) = f(3)\), then these points are symmetric about the vertex.

So, the axis of symmetry (i.e. x-value of vertex) is:

\[ x = \frac{-9 + 3}{2} = -3 \]

Step 2: Use vertex formula to confirm.

The x-coordinate of the vertex of a quadratic is given by:

\[ x = \frac{-b}{2a} \]

Here, \(f(x) = ax^2 + 4x + c\), so:

\[ \frac{-4}{2a} = -3 ⇒ a = \frac{2}{3} \]
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question
slides.html:
Strategy

Label opposite, adjacent, and hypotenuse relative to the given angle.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

In the \(\displaystyle xy\)-plane, a parabola has vertex \(\displaystyle \left(\dfrac{7}{3}, -4\right)\) and intersects the \(\displaystyle x\)-axis at \(\displaystyle (10, 0)\) and \(\displaystyle (a, 0)\), where \(\displaystyle a\) is a constant.

What is the value of \(\displaystyle a\)?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Label opposite, adjacent, and hypotenuse relative to the given angle.

slides.title: Answer
slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Compare your work with the final answer and fix any missed step.

We are given the vertex \(\displaystyle \left(\dfrac{7}{3}, -4\right)\) and the \(\displaystyle x\)-intercepts \(\displaystyle (10, 0)\) and \(\displaystyle (a, 0)\). So the parabola has roots at \(\displaystyle x = 10\) and \(\displaystyle x = a\), and the vertex is at the midpoint of the roots.

Use midpoint formula:

\[ \frac{10 + a}{2} = \frac{7}{3} ⇒ 10 + a = \frac{14}{3} ⇒ a = \frac{14}{3} - 10 = \frac{14 - 30}{3} = -\frac{16}{3} \]
Correct answer
D

Quadratic Formula(X-Intercept)

slides.title: Discriminant of a Quadratic Equation
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

General Form of a Quadratic:

\[ ax^2 + bx + c = 0 \]

Discriminant Formula:

\[ \Delta = b^2 - 4ac \]

Why is the discriminant important?

  • Tells us how many and what type of solutions the quadratic equation has.

Cases:

  • \(\displaystyle \Delta > 0\): Two distinct real solutions
  • \(\displaystyle \Delta = 0\): One real solution (a repeated root)
  • \(\displaystyle \Delta \lt 0\): No real solutions (complex roots)
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

How the Discriminant relates to the graph of \(\displaystyle y = ax^2 + bx + c\):

  • \(\displaystyle \Delta > 0\): The parabola intersects the \(\displaystyle x\)-axis at two points.
  • \(\displaystyle \Delta = 0\): The parabola is tangent to the \(\displaystyle x\)-axis (one point of contact).
  • \(\displaystyle \Delta \lt 0\): The parabola does not intersect the \(\displaystyle x\)-axis.

Implication: You can predict how many real solutions an equation has by simply analyzing its discriminant.
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Question
slides.html:
Strategy

Match coefficients of x and constants on both sides — they must be identical.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

\[ 2x^2 - 13x + 21 = 0 \]

How many distinct real solutions does the given equation have?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Infinite solutions mean the two sides are the same line — all coefficients must match.
slides.strategy_hint: Match coefficients of x and constants on both sides — they must be identical.

slides.title: Answer
slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Compare your work with the final answer and fix any missed step.

We are given the quadratic equation:

\[ 2x^2 - 13x + 21 = 0 \]

Use the discriminant to determine the number of real solutions:

\[ \Delta = b^2 - 4ac = (-13)^2 - 4(2)(21) = 169 - 168 = 1 \]

Since \(\displaystyle \Delta > 0\), the equation has two distinct real solutions.

Correct answer
C

slides.title: Question: Discriminant and No Real Solutions
slides.html:
Strategy

Label opposite, adjacent, and hypotenuse relative to the given angle.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

Consider the quadratic equation:

\[ x^2 - 34x + c = 0 \]

In the given equation, \(c\) is a constant. The equation has no real solutions if \(c > n\).

What is the least possible value of \(n\)?

Your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Label opposite, adjacent, and hypotenuse relative to the given angle.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Using the Discriminant
slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Compare your work with the final answer and fix any missed step.

A quadratic equation \(ax^2 + bx + c = 0\) has:

  • Two real solutions if \(b^2 - 4ac > 0\)
  • One real solution if \(b^2 - 4ac = 0\)
  • No real solution if \(b^2 - 4ac \lt 0\)

In our equation:

\[ x^2 - 34x + c = 0 ⇒ a = 1, b = -34, c = c \]

Compute the discriminant:

\[ \Delta = (-34)^2 - 4(1)(c) = 1156 - 4c \]

Set discriminant to be less than 0:

\[1156 - 4c \lt 0 ⇒ 4c > 1156 ⇒ c > 289\]

Therefore, the equation has no real solutions when:

\[ c > 289 ⇒ \boxed{n = 289} \]
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: The Quadratic Formula
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

To solve any quadratic equation:

\[ ax^2 + bx + c = 0 \]

Use the Quadratic Formula:

\[ x = \frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a} \]

Steps to Use the Formula:

  1. Identify \(\displaystyle a\), \(\displaystyle b\), and \(\displaystyle c\) from the equation.
  2. Plug the values into the formula.
  3. Simplify the square root (use the discriminant).
  4. Solve for both values using \(\displaystyle +\) and \(\displaystyle -\).

Note: Always check the discriminant before solving to understand the type of roots.
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Quadratic Equation with Radical Solutions
slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Compare your work with the final answer and fix any missed step.

Correct Answer: D: \( \frac{1 + \sqrt{11}}{2} \)

✦
Your turnWork it out on paper first — reveal when you're ready.

Solution:

  • Start by rearranging the equation:
    \[ 2x^2 - 2 = 2x + 3 ⇒ 2x^2 - 2x - 5 = 0 \]
  • Use the quadratic formula:
    \[ x = \frac{-(-2) \pm \sqrt{(-2)^2 - 4(2)(-5)}}{2(2)} = \frac{2 \pm \sqrt{4 + 40}}{4} = \frac{2 \pm \sqrt{44}}{4} \]
  • Therefore, the solutions are:
    \[ x = \frac{1 + \sqrt{11}}{2}, x = \frac{1 - \sqrt{11}}{2} \]
Final Answer
\(\boxed{\frac{1 + \sqrt{11}}{2}}\)

slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Correct Answer: C: \(20\)

✦
Your turnWork it out on paper first — reveal when you're ready.

Solution:

  • Use the quadratic formula:
    \[ x = \frac{2 \pm \sqrt{(-2)^2 - 4(1)(-9)}}{2(1)} = \frac{2 \pm \sqrt{4 + 36}}{2} = \frac{2 \pm \sqrt{40}}{2} \]

slides.title: Question: Smallest Solution to a Radical Equation
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify the initial value, growth or decay factor, and how the exponent scales with time.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

Solve the equation:

\[ \sqrt{(x - 2)^2} = \sqrt{3x + 34} \]

What is the smallest solution to the given equation?

Your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify the initial value, growth or decay factor, and how the exponent scales with time.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Solving Radical Equation
slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Compare your work with the final answer and fix any missed step.

Square both sides:

\[ (x - 2)^2 = 3x + 34 ⇒ x^2 - 4x + 4 = 3x + 34 ⇒ x^2 - 7x - 30 = 0 ⇒ (x - 10)(x + 3) = 0 ⇒ x = 10 \text{or} x = -3 \]

Now check for extraneous solutions by plugging into the original equation:

  • For \(x = 10\):
\[ \sqrt{(10 - 2)^2} = \sqrt{3(10) + 34} ⇒ \sqrt{64} = \sqrt{64} \text{✅ valid} \]
  • For \(x = -3\):
\[ \sqrt{(-3 - 2)^2} = \sqrt{3(-3) + 34} ⇒ \sqrt{25} = \sqrt{25} \text{✅ valid} \]

Case 2: \(-(x - 2) = \sqrt{3x + 34}\)

This will yield the same roots already considered since squaring removes signs.

Final Answer
\(\boxed{-3}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question: Solving a Radical Equation
slides.html:
Strategy

Isolate the radical, square both sides if needed, then check for extraneous solutions.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

Solve the equation:

\[ \sqrt{2x + 6} + 4 = x + 3 \]

What is the solution set?

Options:

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Isolate the radical, square both sides if needed, then check for extraneous solutions.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Solving Radical Equation
slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Compare your work with the final answer and fix any missed step.

We start with the given equation:

\[ \sqrt{2x + 6} + 4 = x + 3 \]

Subtract 4 from both sides:

\[ \sqrt{2x + 6} = x - 1 \]

Now square both sides:

\[ 2x + 6 = (x - 1)^2 ⇒ 2x + 6 = x^2 - 2x + 1 \]

Move all terms to one side:

\[ 0 = x^2 - 4x - 5 ⇒ (x - 5)(x + 1) = 0 ⇒ x = 5 \text{or} x = -1 \]
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Solving Radical Equation
slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Compare your work with the final answer and fix any missed step.

Check for extraneous solutions:

For \(x = 5\):

\[ \sqrt{2(5) + 6} + 4 = \sqrt{16} + 4 = 4 + 4 = 8 \text{and} x + 3 = 8 ⇒ \text{✅ valid} \]

For \(x = -1\):

\[ \sqrt{2(-1) + 6} + 4 = \sqrt{4} + 4 = 2 + 4 = 6 \text{and} -1 + 3 = 2 ⇒ \text{❌ not valid} \]
Final Answer
\(\boxed{\{5\}}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?
