slides.title: Up next Introduction to Percent
slides.html:
01Up next

Introduction to Percent

slides.title: Introduction to Percent
slides.html:
Knowledge PointLearn the rule

Understand the definition, formula, or theorem before trying a problem.

Core concept

Understand this definition — every example and practice problem in this lesson builds on it.

Definition: Percent means "per hundred." It represents a ratio of a number to 100.

\[ \text{Percent} = \frac{\text{Part}}{\text{Whole}} \times 100 \]

Example: 25 out of 100 is written as 25%.
slides.study_tip: Focus on definitions and vocabulary — SAT questions often test whether you recognize the form of an equation.

slides.title: Percent Change Formula
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

Formula:

\[ \text{Percent Change} = \frac{\text{New Value} - \text{Original Value}}{\text{Original Value}} \times 100 \]

Example: If a price increases from $80 to $90:

\[ \frac{90 - 80}{80} \times 100 = 12.5\% \]

Key Concept: Positive percent change indicates an increase, negative indicates a decrease.
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Up next Percent Greater or Less Than
slides.html:
02Up next

Percent Greater or Less Than

slides.title: Percent Greater or Less Than
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

Translates to...Example
\(\displaystyle p\%\) of \(\displaystyle x\)\(\displaystyle \dfrac{p}{100} \times x\)\(\displaystyle 15\%\) of \(\displaystyle x ⇒ \dfrac{15}{100} \times x = 0.15x\)
\(\displaystyle p\%\) greater than \(\displaystyle x\)\(\displaystyle \left(1 + \dfrac{p}{100} \right)x\)\(\displaystyle 15\%\) greater than \(\displaystyle x ⇒ \left(1 + \dfrac{15}{100} \right)x = 1.15x\)
\(\displaystyle p\%\) less than \(\displaystyle x\)\(\displaystyle \left(1 - \dfrac{p}{100} \right)x\)\(\displaystyle 15\%\) less than \(\displaystyle x ⇒ \left(1 - \dfrac{15}{100} \right)x = 0.85x\)

Example: If 200 is x% greater than 50, what is x?

\[ 50\times(1+x\%)=200 \]

Key Takeaway: If something is 50% greater than a value, multiply by 1.5.
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Example of the Percent
slides.html:
Strategy

Mark known angles and side relationships first, then use triangle or parallel-line facts.

Guided ExampleWatch the process

Follow the model first, then try the next similar problem yourself.

The number \( a \) is 70% less than the positive number \( b \).

The number \( c \) is 60% greater than \( a \).

The number \( c \) is how many times \( b \)?

Your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Mark known angles and side relationships first, then use triangle or parallel-line facts.

slides.title: Answer
slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Compare your work with the final answer and fix any missed step.

  • Since \( a \) is 70% less than \( b \), we express it as:
    \[ a = (1 - 0.7)b = 0.3b \]
  • Since \( c \) is 60% greater than \( a \), we express it as:
    \[ c = (1 + 0.6)a = 1.6a \]
  • Substituting \( a = 0.3b \):
    \[ c = 1.6(0.3b) = 0.48b \]
  • Thus, the number \( c \) is \(\mathbf{0.48}\) times \( b \).
Final Answer
0.48
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Key Takeaways
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

  • Percent represents "per hundred."
  • Use the percent change formula to calculate increases and decreases.
  • "Percent greater than" and "percent less than" refer to the original value.
  • Use division to find the original value when given the increased price.
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Example of the Percent
slides.html:
Strategy

Mark known angles and side relationships first, then use triangle or parallel-line facts.

Guided ExampleWatch the process

Follow the model first, then try the next similar problem yourself.

The regular price of a shirt at a store is \(\mathbf{$11.70}\).

The sale price of the shirt is 80% less than the regular price,

and the sale price is 30% greater than the store’s cost for the shirt.

What was the store’s cost, in dollars, for the shirt?

(Disregard the $ sign when entering your answer.)

Your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Mark known angles and side relationships first, then use triangle or parallel-line facts.

slides.title: Answer
slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Compare your work with the final answer and fix any missed step.

  • Step 1: Find the sale price of the shirt
    \[ \text{Sale Price} = (1 - 0.80) \times 11.70 = 0.20 \times 11.70 = 2.34 \]
  • Step 2: Set up equation for the store’s cost
    \[ \text{Sale Price} = (1 + 0.30) \times \text{Cost} \]
    \[ 2.34 = 1.30 \times \text{Cost} \]
  • Step 3: Solve for Cost
    \[ \text{Cost} = \frac{2.34}{1.30} = 1.80 \]
Final Answer
1.80
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Up next “Increased By” vs. “Decreased By
slides.html:
03Up next

“Increased By” vs. “Decreased By

slides.title: ``Increased By'' vs. ``Decreased By''
slides.html:
Knowledge PointLearn the rule

Understand the definition, formula, or theorem before trying a problem.

Core concept

Understand this definition — every example and practice problem in this lesson builds on it.

Definitions:

  • Increased by \(\displaystyle p\%\): Multiply by \(\displaystyle \left(1 + \frac{p}{100}\right)\)
  • Decreased by \(\displaystyle p\%\): Multiply by \(\displaystyle \left(1 - \frac{p}{100}\right)\)

Examples:

  • \(\displaystyle 120\) increased by \(\displaystyle 25\%\):
    \[ 120 \times \left(1 + \frac{25}{100}\right) = 120 \times 1.25 = 150 \]
  • \(\displaystyle 150\) decreased by \(\displaystyle 20\%\):
    \[ 150 \times \left(1 - \frac{20}{100}\right) = 150 \times 0.8 = 120 \]
slides.study_tip: Focus on definitions and vocabulary — SAT questions often test whether you recognize the form of an equation.

slides.title: Important Notes
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

Important Notes:

  • Be careful: A 20% decrease followed by a 20% increase does not return to the original value!
    \[ 100 \to 80 \to 96 \]
  • Always treat ``increased by'' and ``decreased by'' as multiplicative — not additive.
  • If you are given a final value and percentage, reverse the multiplier to solve for the original.

Key Insight: Think in terms of scaling, not just adding or subtracting!
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Question: Net Percentage Increase
slides.html:
Strategy

Translate the percent change into a multiplier, then match the time unit in the exponent.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

The value of a collectible comic book increased by \(167\%\) from the end of 2011 to the end of 2012, and then decreased by \(16\%\) from the end of 2012 to the end of 2013.

What was the net percentage increase in the value of the collectible comic book from the end of 2011 to the end of 2013?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Translate the percent change into a multiplier, then match the time unit in the exponent.

slides.title: Answer and Explanation
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

Let the original value at the end of 2011 be \(100\) (for simplicity).

  • After a \(167\%\) increase:
    \[ 100 + 1.67 \times 100 = 267 \]
  • After a \(16\%\) decrease:
    \[ 267 \times (1 - 0.16) = 267 \times 0.84 = 224.28 \]
  • Net increase from original \(100\):
    \[ 224.28 - 100 = 124.28 ⇒ \text{Net increase is } \boxed{124.28\%} \]

Correct Answer: A. \(124.28\%\)
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Up next Practice
slides.html:
04Up next

Practice

slides.title: Percent Greater Relationship
slides.html:
Strategy

Translate the percent change into a multiplier, then match the time unit in the exponent.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

In 2010, the number of houses built in Town A was \(\displaystyle n\) percent greater than the number of houses built in Town B. If \(\displaystyle 700\) houses were built in Town A in 2010, which expression represents the number of houses built in Town B in 2010?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Translate the percent change into a multiplier, then match the time unit in the exponent.

slides.title: Answer
slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Compare your work with the final answer and fix any missed step.

Let \(\displaystyle x\) be the number of houses in Town B.

Town A had \(\displaystyle n\%\) more than Town B, so:

\[ 700 = x \left( \dfrac{100 + n}{100} \right) ⇒ x = 700 \cdot \left( \dfrac{100}{100 + n} \right) \]
Correct answer
B \(700 \left( \dfrac{100}{100 + n} \right)\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Percent Relationship Problem
slides.html:
Strategy

Translate the percent change into a multiplier, then match the time unit in the exponent.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

The sum of positive numbers \(\displaystyle a\) and \(\displaystyle b\) is \(\displaystyle 4.76\) times the positive number \(\displaystyle c\), and \(\displaystyle a\) is \(\displaystyle 124\%\) of \(\displaystyle b\). What percent of \(\displaystyle c\) is \(\displaystyle a\)?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Translate the percent change into a multiplier, then match the time unit in the exponent.

slides.title: Answer
slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Compare your work with the final answer and fix any missed step.

We are given:

\[ a + b = 4.76c \text{and} a = 1.24b \]

Substitute \(\displaystyle a\) into the sum:

\[ 1.24b + b = 4.76c ⇒ 2.24b = 4.76c ⇒ b = \dfrac{4.76c}{2.24} \]

Now substitute \(\displaystyle b\) into \(\displaystyle a = 1.24b\):

\[ a = 1.24 \cdot \dfrac{4.76c}{2.24} = \dfrac{1.24 \cdot 4.76}{2.24}c = 2.635c \]

Convert to percent:

\[ \boxed{a = 263.5\% \text{ of } c} \]
Correct answer
C \(263.50\%\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Percent Relationship Problem
slides.html:
Strategy

Translate the percent change into a multiplier, then match the time unit in the exponent.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

The sum of positive numbers \(\displaystyle x\) and \(\displaystyle y\) is \(\displaystyle 3.5\) times the positive number \(\displaystyle z\), and \(\displaystyle x\) is \(\displaystyle 160\%\) of \(\displaystyle y\). What percent of \(\displaystyle z\) is \(\displaystyle x\)?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Translate the percent change into a multiplier, then match the time unit in the exponent.

slides.title: Answer
slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Compare your work with the final answer and fix any missed step.

We are given:

\[ x + y = 3.5z \text{and} x = 1.6y \]

Substitute \(\displaystyle x\) into the sum:

\[ 1.6y + y = 3.5z ⇒ 2.6y = 3.5z ⇒ y = \dfrac{3.5z}{2.6} \]

Now substitute \(\displaystyle y\) into \(\displaystyle x = 1.6y\):

\[ x = 1.6 \cdot \dfrac{3.5z}{2.6} = \dfrac{1.6 \cdot 3.5}{2.6}z = \dfrac{5.6}{2.6}z \approx 2.1538z \]

Convert to percent:

\[ x \approx 215.38\% \text{ of } z \]
Closest answer
A 212.00%
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Up next Mean, Median, and Mode
slides.html:
01Up next

Mean, Median, and Mode

slides.title: Understanding Mean, Median, and Mode
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

Mean (Average):

\[ \text{Mean} = \frac{x_1 + x_2 + \cdots + x_n}{n} \]
  • Effect of Outliers: Extremely sensitive to outliers.
  • Interpretation: Represents a balance point of the data.
  • If one value increases by \(\displaystyle k\), the mean increases by \(\displaystyle \frac{k}{n}\).

Median (Middle Value):

  • The value separating the higher half from the lower half.
  • Robustness: Resistant to outliers; a better measure of central tendency for skewed data.
  • A single extreme value has no effect unless it shifts the order.
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: More on Mean and Median with Data Behavior
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

When is Median Preferred over Mean?

  • Skewed data (e.g., income distribution).
  • Presence of outliers or extreme values.

Examples of Change Impact:

  • Add 1000 to an income of \(\displaystyle 30,000\): Mean jumps; median may stay same.
  • Remove a high outlier: Mean drops; median may remain unchanged.

Mode:

  • The value(s) that appear most frequently in a data set.
  • May have none, one, or multiple modes.
  • Useful for categorical or discrete data.
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Data Set and Mean Problem
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

Data set \( A \) consists of 10 positive integers less than 60. The list shown gives 9 of the integers from data set \( A \):

\[ 43,\ 45,\ 44,\ 43,\ 38,\ 39,\ 40,\ 46,\ 40 \]

The mean of these 9 integers is 42. If the mean of data set \( A \) is an integer that is greater than 42, what is the value of the largest integer from data set \( A \)?

Your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Solution to Data Set and Mean Problem
slides.html:
Solution ReviewStudy the reasoning

Read the steps carefully, then summarize the method in your own words.

Let the missing number be \( x \). The sum of the 9 known numbers is:

\[ 43 + 45 + 44 + 43 + 38 + 39 + 40 + 46 + 40 = 378 \]

Mean of all 10 numbers must be an integer greater than 42. So:

\[ \frac{378 + x}{10} > 42 ⇒ 378 + x > 420 ⇒ x > 42 \]

Since the mean must also be an integer, try smallest integer value for the mean greater than 42, which is 43:

\[ \frac{378 + x}{10} = 43 ⇒ 378 + x = 430 ⇒ x = 52 \]
Correct Answer
52
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Effect of Adding an Outlier to a Data Set
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

Data set F consists of 55 integers between 170 and 290. Data set G consists of all the integers in data set F as well as the integer 10.

Which of the following must be less for data set F than for data set G?

  • I. The mean
  • II. The median

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: For multiple choice, eliminate obviously wrong options before doing full algebra.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer and Explanation
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

  • Adding the extreme low value 10 to data set F lowers the mean, so \(\displaystyle mean of G \lt mean of F\). The mean is not less for F than for G.
  • The median is unchanged: 10 is far below the range in set F (170–290), so it does not shift the middle value when all 56 values are ordered.

Conclusion: Neither the mean nor the median is less for data set F than for data set G.

Correct Answer: D. Neither I nor II
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Up next Standard Deviation and Range
slides.html:
02Up next

Standard Deviation and Range

slides.title: Standard Deviation: Definition and Analysis
slides.html:
Knowledge PointLearn the rule

Understand the definition, formula, or theorem before trying a problem.

Core concept

Understand this definition — every example and practice problem in this lesson builds on it.

Definition:

\[ \sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^n (x_i - \mu)^2} \]
  • Measures the spread or dispersion of data around the mean.
  • A small \(\displaystyle \sigma\) indicates data points are close to the mean.
  • A large \(\displaystyle \sigma\) implies data is more spread out.

How It Changes:

  • Adding a constant to all values: \(\displaystyle \sigma\) stays the same.
  • Multiplying all values by a constant \(\displaystyle k\): \(\displaystyle \sigma\) becomes \(\displaystyle |k|\sigma\).
  • Introducing an outlier: \(\displaystyle \sigma\) increases significantly.
slides.study_tip: Focus on definitions and vocabulary — SAT questions often test whether you recognize the form of an equation.

slides.title: Range: Definition and Behavior
slides.html:
Knowledge PointLearn the rule

Understand the definition, formula, or theorem before trying a problem.

Core concept

Understand this definition — every example and practice problem in this lesson builds on it.

Definition:

\[ \text{Range} = \text{Maximum value} - \text{Minimum value} \]

Properties:

  • Measures the total spread of a data set.
  • Only uses the two extreme values; does not reflect the distribution of other data points.
  • Sensitive to outliers — a single large or small value can drastically change the range.

How Range is Affected:

  • Adding a constant to all values: No change in range.
  • Multiplying all values by a positive constant \(\displaystyle k\): Range becomes \(\displaystyle k \cdot (original range)\).
  • Replacing max or min with a more extreme value increases the range.
slides.study_tip: Focus on definitions and vocabulary — SAT questions often test whether you recognize the form of an equation.

slides.title: Modified Data Set and Statistical Measures
slides.html:
Strategy

Label opposite, adjacent, and hypotenuse relative to the given angle.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

A data set of 27 different numbers has a mean of 33 and a median of 33. A new data set is created by adding 7 to each number in the original data set that is greater than the median and subtracting 7 from each number in the original data set that is less than the median.

Which of the following measures does NOT have the same value in both the original and new data sets?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: For multiple choice, eliminate obviously wrong options before doing full algebra.
slides.strategy_hint: Label opposite, adjacent, and hypotenuse relative to the given angle.

slides.title: Answer and Explanation
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

  • The median stays the same, since the middle number (33) is unchanged.
  • The mean and sum are unchanged: every increase of 7 is matched by a decrease of 7.
  • The standard deviation increases, since numbers are being moved further from the center (more spread).

The only measure that does not stay the same is the:

\[ {Correct Answer: D. Standard Deviation} \]
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.section: Frequency Table, Dot Plot, Bar Graphs, and Histogram

slides.title: Dot Plot
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

A dot plot is a simple way to display numerical data using dots. Each dot represents one occurrence of a data value, and the dots are stacked vertically above a number line corresponding to the data values.
slides.section: Frequency Table, Dot Plot, Bar Graphs, and Histogram
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Histogram
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

A Histogram is a type of bar graph that represents the frequency distribution of a dataset. It displays data in intervals (also called bins), where the height of each bar corresponds to the frequency of data within that interval. Unlike bar charts, there are no gaps between the bars in a histogram.
slides.section: Frequency Table, Dot Plot, Bar Graphs, and Histogram
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Box Plot
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

A box plot, also known as a box-and-whisker plot, graphically represents the five-number summary:

\[ \text{Minimum}, Q_1 \ (\text{First Quartile}), \text{Median}, Q_3 \ (\text{Third Quartile}), \text{Maximum} \]
slides.section: Frequency Table, Dot Plot, Bar Graphs, and Histogram
slides.study_tip: Quick recap — make sure you can recall each bullet without looking.

slides.title: Contingency Table (Two-Way Table)
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

A Contingency Table or Two-Way Table summarizes the frequency of occurrences for two categorical variables. It helps analyze the relationship between these variables by organizing data into rows and columns.

The table below shows the relationship between students' Gender and their Preference for Study Subject.

GenderMathScienceHistoryTotal
Male20251560
Female30204090
Total504555150

slides.title: Dot Plot and Data Transformation
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

The dot plot represents the 15 values in data set A. Data set B is created by adding \( 56 \) to each of the values in data set A.

Which of the following correctly compares the medians and the ranges of data sets A and B?

Choose your answer
slides.section: Frequency Table, Dot Plot, Bar Graphs, and Histogram
slides.study_tip: For multiple choice, eliminate obviously wrong options before doing full algebra.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer and Explanation
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

  • Adding the same number to all values in a data set increases the median by that amount.
  • However, the spread (range) remains unchanged because both the maximum and minimum values are increased equally.
  • Thus:
    • Median of B is greater than median of A
    • Range of B is equal to range of A
Correct Answer: C

slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

Question PracticeTry it first

For a certain computer game, individuals receive an integer score that ranges from 2 through 10. The table below shows the frequency distribution of the scores of the 9 players in group A and the 11 players in group B.

ScoreGroup AGroup B
210

514
632
700
802
911

Total911

Question: The median of the scores for group B is how much greater than the median of the scores for group A?

Your answer
slides.section: Frequency Table, Dot Plot, Bar Graphs, and Histogram
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.

slides.title: Answer – Median Comparison
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

To find the medians:

  • Group A (9 players): Median is the 5\textsuperscript{th} value. Sorted values based on frequency:
    \[ 2,\ 3,\ 4,\ 4,\ \boxed{5},\ 6,\ 6,\ 6,\ 9 \]
    Median of Group A = \( \boxed{5} \)
  • Group B (11 players): Median is the 6\textsuperscript{th} value. Sorted values based on frequency:
    \[ 5,\ 5,\ 5,\ 5,\ 6,\ \boxed{6},\ 8,\ 8,\ 9,\ 10,\ 10 \]
    Median of Group B = \( \boxed{6} \)
Final Answer
The median of group B is \(\boxed{1}\) greater than the median of group A.

Data set P: 41, 43, 45, 45, 47, 47, 51, 51, 55

Data set Q: 33, 33, 36, 36, 38, 38, 40, 40, 40

The data values for data sets P and Q are shown. The median of data set P is how much greater than the median of data set Q?

Your answer

slides.title: Answer
slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Compare your work with the final answer and fix any missed step.

Walk through the solution

Step 1. Find the median of data set P:

Data set P (ordered): 41, 43, 45, 45, 47, 47, 51, 51, 55

Median: 47

Step 2. Find the median of data set Q:

Data set Q (ordered): 33, 33, 36, 36, 38, 38, 40, 40, 40

Median: 38

Step 3. Difference:

47 - 38 = 9

Answer
The median of data set P is 9 greater than the median of data set Q.

Price (dollars)Frequency
258

454
5510

If an item with a price of 25 dollars is removed from the shopping cart, what will be the effect on the mean and median price of the items in the shopping cart?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer
slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Compare your work with the final answer and fix any missed step.

Walk through the solution

Step 1. Mean Analysis:

Removing a low-priced item (25) from the dataset will increase the overall mean, because the total sum decreases by less than proportionally compared to the decrease in count.

Step 2. Median Analysis:

Since there are 30 items initially, the median is the average of the 15th and 16th values when ordered. After removing one item priced at 25 (the lowest), there are now 29 items. The new median is the 15th value, which remains at 55.

Conclusion:

The mean will increase, and the median will remain the same.

Answer
D

slides.title: Answer
slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Compare your work with the final answer and fix any missed step.

Walk through the solution

Step 1. Mean of data set A:

\[ \text{Mean} = \frac{(30 \cdot 11) + (50 \cdot 11) + (70 \cdot 11)}{33} = \frac{330 + 550 + 770}{33} = \frac{1650}{33} = 50. \]

Step 2. Compare each option:

  • A: All frequencies are equal, mean = 50 (same as set A).
  • B: More weight on 70 and less on 30, so mean > 50.
  • C: More weight on 30, mean < 50.
  • D: Equal frequencies on 30 and 70 but same as set A on 50; mean = 50.
Answer
B

  • Data set B is a translation of data set A by adding 3 to each value.
  • Therefore, the mean of data set B is 3 more than the mean of data set A.

Step 2. Compare the standard deviations:

  • Adding a constant shifts all data points equally, so standard deviation does not change.
  • Therefore, the standard deviation of data set B is equal to that of data set A.

Conclusion:

  • The mean of data set B is greater than the mean of data set A.
  • The standard deviations of data sets A and B are equal.

C

slides.title: Up next Linear vs Exponential Model
slides.html:
01Up next

Linear vs Exponential Model

slides.title: Linear Models
slides.html:
Knowledge PointLearn the rule

Understand the definition, formula, or theorem before trying a problem.

Core concept

Understand this definition — every example and practice problem in this lesson builds on it.

Definition:

A linear model shows a constant rate of change — the dependent variable increases or decreases by the same amount for every unit increase in the independent variable.

\[ y = mx + b \]

Where:

  • \(\displaystyle m\) is the slope (rate of change)
  • \(\displaystyle b\) is the \(\displaystyle y\)-intercept (starting value)

Key Characteristics:

  • Graph is a straight line.
  • Change is additive.
  • Example: Earning $15/hour → \(\displaystyle y = 15x\)
slides.study_tip: Focus on definitions and vocabulary — SAT questions often test whether you recognize the form of an equation.

slides.title: Exponential Models
slides.html:
Knowledge PointLearn the rule

Understand the definition, formula, or theorem before trying a problem.

Core concept

Understand this definition — every example and practice problem in this lesson builds on it.

Definition:

An exponential model shows a constant percentage rate of change — the quantity increases or decreases by the same proportion over equal intervals.

\[ y = a \cdot b^x \]

Where:

  • \(\displaystyle a\) is the initial value
  • \(\displaystyle b\) is the growth (\(\displaystyle b > 1\)) or decay (\(\displaystyle 0 \lt b \lt 1\)) factor

Key Characteristics:

  • Graph is curved — increasing/decreasing rapidly.
  • Change is multiplicative.
  • Example: Population doubles each hour → \(\displaystyle y = 100 \cdot 2^x\)
slides.study_tip: Focus on definitions and vocabulary — SAT questions often test whether you recognize the form of an equation.

slides.title: Exponential Decay – Interpretation of Parameters
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify the initial value, growth or decay factor, and how the exponent scales with time.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

Question:

The function \( M(t) = 4000(0.6)^{\frac{t}{3}} \) models the mass, in nanograms, of a particle after \( t \) years.

Which of the following is the best interpretation of \( (0.6)^{\frac{t}{3}} \) in this context?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: For multiple choice, eliminate obviously wrong options before doing full algebra.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify the initial value, growth or decay factor, and how the exponent scales with time.

slides.title: Answer: Decay Rate over Time
slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Compare your work with the final answer and fix any missed step.

Explanation:

The exponential function is of the form:

\[ M(t) = 4000 \cdot (0.6)^{\frac{t}{3}}, \]

which means that every 3 years, the mass is multiplied by 0.6 — a 40% decrease from the previous amount.

Correct Answer
D The mass of the particle decreases by 40% every 3 years.
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Comparing Linear vs Exponential Models
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

Linear: Constant difference

Exponential: Constant ratio

FeatureLinearExponential
General Form\(\displaystyle y = mx + b\)\(\displaystyle y = a \cdot b^x\)
Rate of Change\(Constant (add/subtract)\)\(Constant (multiply/divide)\)
Graph ShapeStraight line\(Curve (growth/decay)\)
Example$10 per dayDouble each day
Real-World UseSalaries, feesPopulations, investments

Key Insight:

If the rate of change is constant difference, it's linear.

If the rate of change is constant ratio, it's exponential.

slides.html:
Strategy

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

A population of bacteria is observed to grow as follows:

Time (hours)Population
0100
1200
2400
3800

Question:

Which type of model best describes this data?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify the initial value, growth or decay factor, and how the exponent scales with time.

slides.title: Answer and Explanation
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

Answer
B Exponential Model

Why?

  • The population doubles each hour: \(\displaystyle 100 \rightarrow 200 \rightarrow 400 \rightarrow 800\)
  • This shows a constant ratio (multiply by 2).

Model:

\[ P(t) = 100 \cdot 2^t \]

Conclusion:

Because the change is multiplicative, the best model is exponential.

slides.title: Up next Scatterplots and Regression
slides.html:
02Up next

Scatterplots and Regression

slides.title: Understanding Scatterplots
slides.html:
Knowledge PointLearn the rule

Understand the definition, formula, or theorem before trying a problem.

Core concept

Understand this definition — every example and practice problem in this lesson builds on it.

Definition:

A scatterplot is a graph that displays pairs of data points \(\displaystyle (x, y)\) as dots in the coordinate plane.

Purpose:

  • Visualizes the relationship between two numerical variables.
  • Reveals trends, clusters, outliers, or correlation direction.

Types of Trends:

  • Positive association: As \(\displaystyle x\) increases, \(\displaystyle y\) increases.
  • Negative association: As \(\displaystyle x\) increases, \(\displaystyle y\) decreases.
  • No association: No visible pattern or relationship.

Note: Scatterplots do not imply causation, only correlation.
slides.study_tip: Focus on definitions and vocabulary — SAT questions often test whether you recognize the form of an equation.

slides.title: Linear Regression with Scatterplots
slides.html:
Knowledge PointLearn the rule

Understand the definition, formula, or theorem before trying a problem.

Core concept

Understand this definition — every example and practice problem in this lesson builds on it.

Definition:

A linear regression is a line of best fit through a scatterplot that models the linear relationship between variables.

Equation of the line:

\[ y = mx + b \]

where:

  • \(\displaystyle m\) is the slope (rate of change),
  • \(\displaystyle b\) is the \(\displaystyle y\)-intercept (value of \(\displaystyle y\) when \(\displaystyle x = 0\)).

Properties:

  • Minimizes the sum of squared vertical distances (residuals).
  • Used for prediction and modeling trends.
  • The closer the data to the line, the stronger the linear relationship.

Tip: Use a calculator or software to find the line of best fit and correlation coefficient \(\displaystyle r\).
slides.study_tip: Focus on definitions and vocabulary — SAT questions often test whether you recognize the form of an equation.

slides.html:
Strategy

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

The scatterplot below shows a company's ice cream sales \( d \), in dollars, and the high temperature \( t \), in degrees Celsius (°C), on 12 different days. A line of best fit for the data is also shown.

Which of the following could be an equation of the line of best fit?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: For multiple choice, eliminate obviously wrong options before doing full algebra.
slides.strategy_hint: Mark known angles and side relationships first, then use triangle or parallel-line facts.

slides.title: Answer: Line of Best Fit Interpretation
slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Compare your work with the final answer and fix any missed step.

From the scatterplot:

  • The line of best fit appears to pass through approximately \( (12, 480) \) and \( (24, 876) \)
  • Estimating the slope:
    \[ \text{slope} = \frac{876 - 480}{24 - 12} = \frac{396}{12} = 33 \]
  • Plug into point-slope form:
    \[ d - 480 = 33(t - 12) ⇒ d = 33t + 84 \]
Correct Answer
D \(d = 33t + 84\)

Probability

slides.title: Definition of Probability
slides.html:
Knowledge PointLearn the rule

Understand the definition, formula, or theorem before trying a problem.

Core concept

Understand this definition — every example and practice problem in this lesson builds on it.

Probability is a measure of how likely an event is to occur. It is defined as:

\[ P(\text{Event}) = \frac{\text{Number of favorable outcomes}}{\text{Total number of possible outcomes}} \]

Key Properties:

  • \(\displaystyle 0 \leq P(A) \leq 1\) for any event \(\displaystyle A\)
  • \(\displaystyle P(certain event) = 1\)
  • \(\displaystyle P(impossible event) = 0\)
  • \(\displaystyle P(not A) = 1 - P(A)\)
  • For mutually exclusive events \(\displaystyle A\) and \(\displaystyle B\):
    \[ P(A \text{ or } B) = P(A) + P(B) \]
slides.study_tip: Focus on definitions and vocabulary — SAT questions often test whether you recognize the form of an equation.

slides.title: Simple Probability Examples
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

Example 1: A fair die is rolled. What is the probability of getting an even number?

\[ \text{Even numbers: } \{2, 4, 6\} ⇒ \text{Favorable outcomes: } 3 \]
\[ \text{Total outcomes: } 6 ⇒ P(\text{even}) = \frac{3}{6} = \frac{1}{2} \]

Example 2: A card is drawn from a standard deck. What is the probability of drawing a heart?

\[ \text{Hearts: } 13, \text{Total cards: } 52 ⇒ P(\text{heart}) = \frac{13}{52} = \frac{1}{4} \]
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Conditional Probability (Concept)
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

Conditional Probability is the probability of event \(\displaystyle A\) occurring given that event \(\displaystyle B\) has occurred.

\[ P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)} \]

Key Points:

  • \(\displaystyle P(A \mid B)\) narrows the sample space to only outcomes where \(\displaystyle B\) happens.
  • Only defined if \(\displaystyle P(B) \ne 0\).

Visual:

Think of "restricting" the universe to \(\displaystyle B\), and checking how many of those are also \(\displaystyle A\)
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Probability - Score Distribution Table
slides.html:
Strategy

Label opposite, adjacent, and hypotenuse relative to the given angle.

Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

The same 20 contestants, on each of 3 days, answered 5 questions in order to win a prize. Each contestant received 1 point for each correct answer. The number of contestants receiving a given score on each day is shown in the table below.

ScoreDay 1Day 2Day 3Total
5 out of 52237
4 out of 53339
3 out of 545313
2 out of 565415
1 out of 52439
0 out of 53126
Total20202060

No contestant received the same score on two different days. If a contestant is selected at random, what is the probability that the selected contestant received a score of 5 on Day 2 or Day 3, given that the contestant received a score of 5 on one of the three days?

Your answer

Enter an exact fraction or decimal equivalent.
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Label opposite, adjacent, and hypotenuse relative to the given angle.

slides.title: Answer: Conditional Probability
slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Compare your work with the final answer and fix any missed step.

Walk through the solution

Problem Context:

  • 20 contestants each day, over 3 days (no repeat scores).
  • Table shows number of contestants scoring 0–5 on each day.
  • Total scores of 5 across all days: \(\displaystyle 2 + 2 + 3 = 7\)

Given: A contestant received a score of \(\displaystyle 5\) on one of the days.

Find: Probability that this contestant scored \(\displaystyle 5\) on Day 2 or Day 3.

Step 1: Count favorable outcomes

\[ \text{Day 2: } 2 \qquad \text{Day 3: } 3 \qquad \text{Total favorable} = 2 + 3 = 5 \]

Step 2: Count total outcomes

\[ \text{Total contestants with score of 5: } 7 \]
Answer
\[ P(\text{Day 2 or Day 3} \mid \text{Score 5}) = \frac{5}{7} \]
Final Answer
\(\frac{5}{7}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Up next Inference from Sample Statistics
slides.html:
01Up next

Inference from Sample Statistics

slides.title: What is Inference from Sample Statistics?
slides.html:
Knowledge PointLearn the rule

Understand the definition, formula, or theorem before trying a problem.

Core concept

Understand this definition — every example and practice problem in this lesson builds on it.

Definition:

Inference refers to the process of using data from a sample to draw conclusions about a population.

Why do we use samples?

  • Studying an entire population is often impractical or impossible.
  • A well-designed sample provides useful, reliable insight.

Example:

If we randomly sample 200 voters and 65% support a policy, we can estimate the population support is around 65%.

Important: Conclusions are not certain — they are probabilistic.
slides.study_tip: Focus on definitions and vocabulary — SAT questions often test whether you recognize the form of an equation.

slides.title: Key Properties and Considerations
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

1. Random Sampling:

A sample must be random to avoid bias and make valid inferences.

2. Sample Size:

Larger samples tend to yield more accurate estimates (smaller margin of error).

3. Variability:

Different random samples produce slightly different statistics. This is expected.

4. Population vs. Sample:

  • Parameter – a fixed value describing the population (unknown).
  • Statistic – a computed value from a sample (known).

Notation:

  • Population mean: \(\displaystyle \mu\), Sample mean: \(\displaystyle \bar{x}\)
  • Population proportion: \(\displaystyle p\), Sample proportion: \(\displaystyle \hat{p}\)
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Question: Predicting Election Outcome from Poll Data
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

The table shows the results of a poll. A total of \( 803 \) voters selected at random were asked which candidate they would vote for in the upcoming election.

CandidateVotes
Angel Cruz483
Terry Smith320

According to the poll, if \( 6,424 \) people vote in the election, by how many votes would Angel Cruz be expected to win?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer: Calculating Predicted Vote Margin
slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Compare your work with the final answer and fix any missed step.

Walk through the solution

Step 1: Compute proportions of votes from the poll.

\[ \text{Angel Cruz's proportion: } \frac{483}{803}, \text{Terry Smith's proportion: } \frac{320}{803} \]

Step 2: Apply these proportions to the total of \( 6,424 \) voters.

\[ \text{Votes for Cruz: } \frac{483}{803} \times 6424 \approx 3864.01 \]
\[ \text{Votes for Smith: } \frac{320}{803} \times 6424 \approx 2560.00 \]

Step 3: Subtract to find the expected margin.

\[ 3864 - 2560 = \boxed{1304} \]
Correct Answer
B 1,304
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Up next Margin of Error
slides.html:
02Up next

Margin of Error

slides.title: Understanding Margin of Error
slides.html:
Knowledge PointLearn the rule

Understand the definition, formula, or theorem before trying a problem.

Core concept

Understand this definition — every example and practice problem in this lesson builds on it.

Definition:

The margin of error (MOE) is the maximum expected difference between the true population parameter and a sample estimate.

General Formula:

\[ \text{Margin of Error} = z^* \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \]

or for proportions:

\[ \text{MOE} = z^* \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\hat{p}(1 - \hat{p})}{n}} \]

Where:

  • \(\displaystyle z^*\) is the z-score based on confidence level (e.g., 1.96 for 95%),
  • \(\displaystyle \sigma\) is the population standard deviation (or estimate),
  • \(\displaystyle n\) is the sample size,
  • \(\displaystyle \hat{p}\) is the sample proportion.
slides.study_tip: Focus on definitions and vocabulary — SAT questions often test whether you recognize the form of an equation.

slides.title: Properties of Margin Error
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

Properties:

Properties
PropertySmaller MOE = more precise estimate.
PropertyLarger MOE = more uncertainty.

Factors that Affect Margin of Error:

  • Sample size (\(\displaystyle n\)): Larger \(\displaystyle n\) → smaller MOE.
  • Confidence level: Higher confidence → larger \(\displaystyle z^*\) → larger MOE.
  • Population variability: More variation → larger MOE.

Key Idea:

To decrease MOE, increase the sample size or reduce the confidence level.
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Question: Interpreting Margin of Error
slides.html:
Strategy

Translate the percent change into a multiplier, then match the time unit in the exponent.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

In a study of cell phone use, 799 randomly selected US teens were asked how often they talked on a cell phone and about their texting behavior. The data are summarized in the table below.

Texting behaviorTalks on cell phone dailyDoes not talk on cell phone daily
Light110146
Medium139164
Heavy16674
Total415384

Based on the data from the study, an estimate of the percent of US teens who are heavy texters is \( 30\% \), and the associated margin of error is \( 3\% \). Which of the following is a correct statement based on the given margin of error?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: For multiple choice, eliminate obviously wrong options before doing full algebra.
slides.strategy_hint: Translate the percent change into a multiplier, then match the time unit in the exponent.

slides.title: Answer: Margin of Error Interpretation
slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Compare your work with the final answer and fix any missed step.

Estimated percent: \( 30\% \)

Margin of error: \( \pm 3\% \)

\[ \text{Confidence interval: } [30\% - 3\%, 30\% + 3\%] = [27\%, 33\%] \]
  • A. Incorrect: Margin of error relates to population estimates, not misclassification of sample responses.
  • B. Incorrect: While values outside the interval are less likely, we cannot claim impossibility.
  • C. Incorrect: 33% is the upper bound; the true value could be anywhere in the interval.
  • D. Correct: 35% is outside the margin of error range. Therefore, it is doubtful that the true percentage is as high as 35%.
Correct Answer
\(\boxed{D}\)

Explanation:

Margin of error decreases as sample size increases. Since both samples were selected from the same population and analyzed using the same method, the greater margin of error in Sample A suggests it had a smaller sample size.

slides.html:
Strategy

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

In a psychological study, 600 college gymnasts were randomly selected and put in high-stakes competitions to determine whether they performed better under pressure. Based on the results of this study, it is estimated that 65% of all college gymnasts perform better under pressure, with an associated margin of error of 4%. Which of the following inferences can appropriately be drawn based on the given margin of error?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: For multiple choice, eliminate obviously wrong options before doing full algebra.
slides.strategy_hint: Translate the percent change into a multiplier, then match the time unit in the exponent.

slides.title: Answer
slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Compare your work with the final answer and fix any missed step.

Walk through the solution

Step 1. The estimate is 65% with a margin of error of 4%. So the confidence interval is:

\[ 65\% \pm 4\% = (61\%, 69\%). \]

Step 2. Analyze the choices:

  • A: Incorrect, as 65% to 69% does not include the lower bound (61%).
  • B: Incorrect interpretation of margin of error; it doesn't refer to individual gymnasts.
  • C: Correct—60% is outside the confidence interval, making it unlikely.
  • D: Incorrect—70% is outside the confidence interval but not strictly impossible.
Answer
C
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

Two candidates are running for governor of a state. A recent poll reports that out of a random sample of 250 voters, 110 support candidate A and 140 support candidate B. An estimated 500,000 state residents are expected to vote on election day. According to the poll, candidate B is expected to receive how many more votes than candidate A?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer
slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Compare your work with the final answer and fix any missed step.

Walk through the solution

Step 1. Find the proportion supporting each candidate in the sample:

\[ \text{Candidate A: } \frac{110}{250} = 0.44 \text{Candidate B: } \frac{140}{250} = 0.56. \]

Step 2. Find the expected number of votes for each candidate:

\[ \text{Candidate A: } 500,000 \times 0.44 = 220,000 \]
\[ \text{Candidate B: } 500,000 \times 0.56 = 280,000. \]

Step 3. Calculate the difference:

\[ 280,000 - 220,000 = 60,000. \]
Answer
A

Explanation:

Since only about 10% of the residents responded—and those who chose to respond might have stronger opinions or more vested interest—this is an example of voluntary response bias. This means the results are not representative of all town residents.

01Up next

Observation Study and Experiments

slides.title: Population vs. Sample
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

Population:

The entire group you want information about.

Sample:

A subset of the population used to make inferences.

Goal of Statistical Reasoning:

Use data from a sample to draw conclusions about the population.

  • Representative Sample: Accurately reflects the entire population.
  • Random Sampling: Each member of the population has an equal chance of being selected.
  • Sample Size: Larger samples reduce variability and give more accurate estimates.

⚠️ Caution: A biased or small sample can lead to invalid conclusions.
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Observational Studies vs. Experiments
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

Observational Study:

Researchers observe outcomes without assigning treatments.

Experiment:

Researchers impose a treatment to measure its effect.

Key Differences:

  • Observational Study: Can identify associations, but not causation.
  • Experiment: Can show causation if well-designed (randomized, controlled).

Tip:

If the study doesn’t include random assignment or control groups, it's likely observational — so don’t claim causation.
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Differences Between Sample Survey, Observational Study, and Experiment
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

Differences:

  • Sample Survey: Collects data by asking questions, no control over variables.
  • Observational Study: Observes subjects without manipulating them.
  • Experiment: Researcher manipulates variables and assigns treatments to test cause-and-effect.
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Bias in Sampling and Survey Design
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

Bias: A systematic error that leads to inaccurate or misleading results.

Types of Bias to Watch For:

  • Sampling Bias: Sample does not represent the population.
  • Nonresponse Bias: Selected individuals do not respond.
  • Voluntary Response Bias: Participants self-select, often leading to extreme views.
  • Question Wording Bias: Leading or confusing questions distort responses.

SAT Alert:

If the question hints that the sample was not random or the survey was biased — the conclusions are not reliable.
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Correct Answer
B All fourth-grade students at the school.

Explanation:

Because the 40 students were selected at random from the population of fourth-grade students at the school, the results can be generalized to that group—but not beyond it.

slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Correct Answer
B Those who responded to the poll were not a random sample of the population of the United States.

Explanation:

Because the poll was open only to viewers of the show who chose to respond, the respondents are not a random or representative sample of the broader US population.

A researcher wanted to determine whether studying at a library is more effective than studying at home. He selected a random sample of 200 students and gave them three days to study for the same test. The 100 students who lived closest to a library were asked to study at a library, and the remaining 100 students were asked to study at home. After the students took the test, the scores of those who studied at a library were compared to the scores of those who studied at home. How should this experiment be changed so that the researcher can conclude whether studying at a library is more effective than studying at home?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Rewrite the equation in standard form to read off the center and radius.

slides.title: Answer
slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Compare your work with the final answer and fix any missed step.

Explanation:

The current design introduces confounding because the assignment to study location is based on proximity to a library, which could be correlated with other factors (e.g. socioeconomic status, prior academic experience). Random assignment to study location would eliminate this confounding variable and allow the researcher to isolate the effect of study location.

Answer
B

A researcher conducted a study to determine if certain memorization strategies could improve the performance of chess players. For the study, the researcher selected a random sample of adults who are competing in a chess tournament. At the end of the study, 80% of those sampled reported that they did not find the memorization strategies to be useful during their chess games. Which of the following is the largest population to which the results of this study can be applied?

choices.text: All chess players who compete in tournaments.
All adults who compete in chess tournaments.
All chess players competing in the tournament.
All adults competing in the tournament.

checkpoint.title: Question

A group of school administrators wanted to gather opinions on new teacher certification requirements that were introduced in a certain state. The group surveyed a random sample of math teachers from one of the school districts in the state. The survey showed that the majority of the teachers in the sample support the new requirements. Which of the following is the largest population to which the results of the survey can be generalized?

choices.text: All math teachers in the school district.
All teachers in the school district.
All math teachers in the state.

checkpoint.title: Question

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

A researcher wanted to determine whether studying at a library is more effective than studying at home. He selected a random sample of 200 students and gave them three days to study for the same test. The 100 students who lived closest to a library were asked to study at a library, and the remaining 100 students were asked to study at home. After the students took the test, the scores of those who studied at a library were compared to the scores of those who studied at home. How should this experiment be changed so that the researcher can conclude whether studying at a library is more effective than studying at home?

choices.text: The students assigned to study at a library should study at the same library.
The students should be randomly assigned to study either at a library or at home.
The students assigned to study at a library should take a more difficult test than the students assigned to study at home.
No changes to the experiment are necessary.

A company consists of full-time and part-time employees. Perimeter and Area

slides.title: Perimeter Formulas
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

  • Square: \( P = 4s \)
  • Rectangle: \( P = 2(l + w) \)
  • Triangle: \( P = a + b + c \)
  • Circle (Circumference): \( P = 2\pi r \) or \( P = \pi d \)
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Area Formulas
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

  • Square: \( A = s^2 \)
  • Rectangle: \( A = lw \)
  • Triangle: \( A = \frac{1}{2} bh \)
  • Parallelogram: \( A = bh \)
  • Trapezoid: \( A = \frac{1}{2} (b_1 + b_2) h \)
  • Circle: \( A = \pi r^2 \)
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Up next Volume

02Up next

Volume

slides.title: Prism Volume
slides.html:
Knowledge PointLearn the rule

Understand the definition, formula, or theorem before trying a problem.

Core concept

Understand this definition — every example and practice problem in this lesson builds on it.

Definition: A prism is a three-dimensional shape with two parallel and congruent bases.

The volume of a prism is calculated using the formula:

\[ V_{\text{PRISM}} = Bh \]

where:

  • \( B \) is the Area of the Base.
  • \( h \) is the Height of the Prism (one of its lateral sides).
slides.study_tip: Focus on definitions and vocabulary — SAT questions often test whether you recognize the form of an equation.

slides.title: Surface Area of a Cube
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify the formula, then track how scaling or unit changes affect the result.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

Question:

A cube has a volume of 42,875 cubic inches. What is the surface area, in square inches, of the cube?

Your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify the formula, then track how scaling or unit changes affect the result.

slides.title: Solution
slides.html:
Solution ReviewStudy the reasoning

Read the steps carefully, then summarize the method in your own words.

✦
Your turnWork it out on paper first — reveal when you're ready.

Solution:

  • Let \( s \) be the side length of the cube.
  • Given:
    \[ s^3 = 42,875 \]
  • Solve for \( s \):
    \[ s = \sqrt[3]{42,875} \approx 35 \text{ inches} \]
  • Surface area of the cube:
    \[ \text{Surface Area} = 6s^2 = 6 \times (35)^2 = 6 \times 1225 = 7350 \text{ in}^2 \]
Answer
7350 \) square inches.
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Cylinder Volume Calculation
slides.html:
Knowledge PointLearn the rule

Understand the definition, formula, or theorem before trying a problem.

Core concept

Understand this definition — every example and practice problem in this lesson builds on it.

Definition: A cylinder is a three-dimensional shape with two parallel, congruent circular bases.

The volume of a cylinder is calculated using the formula:

\[ V_{\text{CYLINDER}} = Bh \]
\[ V_{\text{CYLINDER}} = \pi r^2 h \]

where:

  • \( B \) is the Area of the Base (which is a circle, so \( B = \pi r^2 \)).
  • \( h \) is the Height of the Cylinder, the perpendicular distance between the two circular bases.
slides.study_tip: Focus on definitions and vocabulary — SAT questions often test whether you recognize the form of an equation.

slides.title: Cylinder Problem
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify the formula, then track how scaling or unit changes affect the result.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

Question:

A cylinder has a volume of \(980\pi\) cubic feet and a height of 5 feet. What is the base diameter, in feet, of the cylinder?

Your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify the formula, then track how scaling or unit changes affect the result.

slides.title: Solution
slides.html:
Solution ReviewStudy the reasoning

Read the steps carefully, then summarize the method in your own words.

✦
Your turnWork it out on paper first — reveal when you're ready.

Solution:

  • Volume of a cylinder:
    \[ V = \pi r^2 h \]
  • Given:
    \[ 980\pi = \pi r^2 \times 5 \]
  • Simplify:
    \[ r^2 = \frac{980}{5} \]
    \[ r^2 = 196 \]
    \[ r = 14 \text{ feet} \]
  • Diameter:
    \[ d = 2r = 2 \times 14 = 28 \text{ feet} \]
Answer
28\) feet
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Pyramid Volume Calculation
slides.html:
Knowledge PointLearn the rule

Understand the definition, formula, or theorem before trying a problem.

Core concept

Understand this definition — every example and practice problem in this lesson builds on it.

Definition: A pyramid is a three-dimensional shape with a polygonal base and triangular faces that converge at a single point (the apex).

The volume of a pyramid is given by the formula:

\[ V_{\text{PYRAMID}} = \frac{1}{3} Bh \]

where:

  • \( B \) is the Area of the Base.
  • \( h \) is the Height of the Pyramid, the perpendicular distance from the base to the apex.
  • The volume of a pyramid is always one-third of the volume of a prism with the same base and height.
slides.study_tip: Focus on definitions and vocabulary — SAT questions often test whether you recognize the form of an equation.

slides.title: Volume of a Pyramid
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify the formula, then track how scaling or unit changes affect the result.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

Question:

A right square pyramid has a height of 33 centimeters (cm) and a square base with a side length of 14 cm. What is the volume, in cm³, of this pyramid?

Your answer

Use ^ for exponents and / for fractions.
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify the formula, then track how scaling or unit changes affect the result.

slides.title: Solution
slides.html:
Solution ReviewStudy the reasoning

Read the steps carefully, then summarize the method in your own words.

✦
Your turnWork it out on paper first — reveal when you're ready.

Solution:

  • The formula for the volume of a right square pyramid is:
    \[ V = \frac{1}{3} \times \text{Base Area} \times \text{Height} \]
  • Base area:
    \[ \text{Base Area} = 14 \times 14 = 196 \text{ cm}^2 \]
  • Therefore:
    \[ V = \frac{1}{3} \times 196 \times 33 \]
    \[ V = \frac{1}{3} \times 6468 \]
    \[ V = 2156 \text{ cm}^3 \]
Answer
\(2156 cm^3\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Right Triangular Pyramid
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

What is a Right Triangular Pyramid?

  • A three-dimensional solid with a triangular base.
  • The apex (top point) is directly above the centroid of the triangular base.
  • Has 4 faces: 1 triangular base and 3 triangular lateral faces.
  • All lateral edges meet at the apex.
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Right Triangular Pyramid
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

Volume Formula:

\[ V = \frac{1}{3} \times \text{Base Area} \times \text{Height} \]

Where:

  • Height = perpendicular distance from the apex to the base

Surface Area Formula:

\[ \text{Surface Area} = \text{Base Area} + \text{Sum of lateral face areas} \]

Each lateral face is a triangle: \(\displaystyle \frac{1}{2} \times base \times slant height\)
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Up next Density

Density

slides.title: Density
slides.html:
Knowledge PointLearn the rule

Understand the definition, formula, or theorem before trying a problem.

Core concept

Understand this definition — every example and practice problem in this lesson builds on it.

Definition: Density is a measure of how much mass is contained in a given volume.

Formula

\[ \text{Density} = \frac{\text{Mass}}{\text{Volume}} \]

where:

  • Mass is typically measured in grams (g) or kilograms (kg).
  • Volume is typically measured in cubic centimeters (cm³) or cubic meters (m³).

Example:

  • A rock has a mass of 200 grams and a volume of 50 cm³.
  • Calculate the density:
    \[ \text{Density} = \frac{200 \text{ g}}{50 \text{ cm}^3} = 4 \text{ g/cm}^3 \]
slides.study_tip: Focus on definitions and vocabulary — SAT questions often test whether you recognize the form of an equation.

slides.title: Density Problem
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify the formula, then track how scaling or unit changes affect the result.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

Question:

The mass of a foam block is 8 kilograms. The volume of the block is 20 cubic meters. What is the density, in kilograms per cubic meter, of the block?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify the formula, then track how scaling or unit changes affect the result.

slides.title: Solution
slides.html:
Solution ReviewStudy the reasoning

Read the steps carefully, then summarize the method in your own words.

✦
Your turnWork it out on paper first — reveal when you're ready.

Solution:

Density is given by:

\[ \text{Density} = \frac{\text{Mass}}{\text{Volume}} = \frac{8 \text{ kg}}{20 \text{ m}^3} = 0.4 \text{ kg/m}^3. \]
Answer
A
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Up next Ratio of Length, Area, and Volume

Ratio of Length, Area, and Volume
slides.section: Ratio of Length, Area, and Volume

slides.title: Ratio of Length, Area, and Volume
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

Concept:

When a geometric shape is scaled by a factor of \( k \), the following relationships hold:

  • Length Ratio: If the scale factor is \( k \), then all linear measurements (length, width, height, radius, etc.) change by a factor of \( k \).
    \[ \text{New Length} = k \times \text{Original Length} \]
  • Area Ratio: The area of a shape is proportional to the square of the scale factor.
    \[ \text{New Area} = k^2 \times \text{Original Area} \]
  • Volume Ratio: The volume of a three-dimensional shape is proportional to the cube of the scale factor.
    \[ \text{New Volume} = k^3 \times \text{Original Volume} \]
slides.section: Ratio of Length, Area, and Volume
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Question: Ratio of Length, Area, and Volume
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify the formula, then track how scaling or unit changes affect the result.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

Suppose a cube has an original edge length of \( 2 \) cm, and we scale it up by a factor of \( k = 3 \).

How do the length, area, and volume change under this transformation?

Options:

Choose your answer
slides.section: Ratio of Length, Area, and Volume
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify the formula, then track how scaling or unit changes affect the result.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Ratio of Length, Area, and Volume
slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Compare your work with the final answer and fix any missed step.

Correct Answer
\(k, k^2, k^3\)
✦
Your turnWork it out on paper first — reveal when you're ready.

Solution:

When a shape is scaled by a factor of \( k \):

  • Length: Scales by \( k \).
    \[ \text{New Length} = k \times \text{Original Length} \]
  • Area: Scales by \( k^2 \).
    \[ \text{New Area} = k^2 \times \text{Original Area} \]
  • Volume: Scales by \( k^3 \).
    \[ \text{New Volume} = k^3 \times \text{Original Volume} \]
Final Answer
\(\boxed{k, k^2, k^3} \).\)
slides.section: Ratio of Length, Area, and Volume
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question: Finding the Volume of a Cube
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify the formula, then track how scaling or unit changes affect the result.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

A cube has a surface area of \( 54 \) square meters.

What is the volume, in cubic meters, of the cube?

Options:

Choose your answer
slides.section: Ratio of Length, Area, and Volume
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify the formula, then track how scaling or unit changes affect the result.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Finding the Volume of a Cube
slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Compare your work with the final answer and fix any missed step.

Correct Answer
B
✦
Your turnWork it out on paper first — reveal when you're ready.

Solution:

A cube has 6 identical square faces. If the total surface area is given as:

\[ 6s^2 = 54 \]

Solve for \( s \):

\[ s^2 = \frac{54}{6} = 9 \]
\[ s = \sqrt{9} = 3 \]

Now, calculate the volume:

\[ V = s^3 = 3^3 = 27 \]
Final Answer
\(\boxed{27} \).\)
slides.section: Ratio of Length, Area, and Volume
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question: Area of a Scaled Poster
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify the formula, then track how scaling or unit changes affect the result.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

A rectangular poster has an area of 360 square inches.

A copy of the poster is made in which the length and width

of the original poster are each increased by 20%.

What is the area of the copy, in square inches?

Your answer
slides.section: Ratio of Length, Area, and Volume
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify the formula, then track how scaling or unit changes affect the result.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Area of a Scaled Poster
slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Compare your work with the final answer and fix any missed step.

Walk through the solution
✦
Your turnWork it out on paper first — reveal when you're ready.

Solution:

Step 1: Define Scaling Factor

Since both length and width increase by \( 20\% \),

the new dimensions are scaled by:

\[ k = 1.2 \]

Step 2: Apply Area Scaling Formula

Since area scales by \( k^2 \):

\[ \text{New Area} = 360 \times (1.2)^2 \]
\[ = 360 \times 1.44 = 518.4 \]
Final Answer
\(\boxed{518.4}\) \) square inches.
slides.section: Ratio of Length, Area, and Volume
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Cube Problem
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify the formula, then track how scaling or unit changes affect the result.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

Question:

The surface area, in square inches, of a cube can be represented by the expression \(24a^2\), where \(a\) is a positive constant. Which expression represents the volume, in cubic inches, of the cube?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify the formula, then track how scaling or unit changes affect the result.

slides.title: Solution
slides.html:
Solution ReviewStudy the reasoning

Read the steps carefully, then summarize the method in your own words.

✦
Your turnWork it out on paper first — reveal when you're ready.

Solution:

  • Let \(s\) be the side length of the cube.
  • The surface area of the cube is \(6s^2 = 24a^2\).
  • Solve for \(s^2\):
    \[ s^2 = \frac{24a^2}{6} = 4a^2 \]
  • Then:
    \[ s = 2a \]
  • The volume of the cube:
    \[ V = s^3 = (2a)^3 = 8a^3 \]
Answer
\(8a^3\) (Choice A)\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Volume of a Cylinder
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify the formula, then track how scaling or unit changes affect the result.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

Question:

Given the function \( f(x) = \pi x^3 + \pi x^2 \), where \( f \) represents the volume (in cubic feet) of a right circular cylinder with radius \( x \) feet, which expression represents the height (in feet) of the cylinder?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify the formula, then track how scaling or unit changes affect the result.

slides.title: Solution
slides.html:
Solution ReviewStudy the reasoning

Read the steps carefully, then summarize the method in your own words.

✦
Your turnWork it out on paper first — reveal when you're ready.

Solution:

  • The volume of a right circular cylinder is given by:
    \[ V = \pi r^2 h \]
  • Comparing with \( f(x) = \pi x^3 + \pi x^2 \), notice that:
    \[ f(x) = \pi x^2 (x + 1) \]
  • Therefore, \( r = x \) and \( h = x + 1 \).
Answer
\(x + 1 \) (Choice A)\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Rectangle Perimeter Problem
slides.html:
Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

Question:

Identical rectangles \(X\) and \(Y\) each have an area of \(\frac{49}{5}\) square meters and a perimeter of \(P\) meters. If one of the shorter sides of rectangle \(X\) is glued to one of the shorter sides of rectangle \(Y\), the resulting rectangle has a perimeter of \(\frac{14}{9}P\) meters. What is the value of \(P\)?
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify the formula, then track how scaling or unit changes affect the result.

slides.title: Solution
slides.html:
Solution ReviewStudy the reasoning

Read the steps carefully, then summarize the method in your own words.

✦
Your turnWork it out on paper first — reveal when you're ready.

Solution:

  • Let \(l\) and \(w\) be the length and width of each rectangle.
  • Then:
    \[ lw = \frac{49}{5} \text{and} 2(l+w) = P \]
  • When glued along the shorter side:
    \[ \text{New perimeter} = 2(l + 2w) \]
  • Given:
    \[ 2(l + 2w) = \frac{14}{9}P \]
  • But \(P = 2(l+w)\) so:
    \[ 2(l + 2w) = \frac{14}{9} \cdot 2(l+w) \]
  • Dividing both sides by 2:
    \[ l + 2w = \frac{14}{9}(l+w) \]
  • Solve:
    \[ 9(l + 2w) = 14(l + w) \]
    \[ 9l + 18w = 14l + 14w \]
    \[ 4w = 5l \]
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Solution
slides.html:
Solution ReviewStudy the reasoning

Read the steps carefully, then summarize the method in your own words.

  • From \(lw = \frac{49}{5}\):
    \[ l \left(\frac{5l}{4}\right) = \frac{49}{5} \]
    \[ \frac{5l^2}{4} = \frac{49}{5} \]
    \[ l^2 = \frac{49}{5} \cdot \frac{4}{5} = \frac{196}{25} \]
    \[ l = \frac{14}{5} \]
  • Then:
    \[ w = \frac{5l}{4} = \frac{5 \cdot \frac{14}{5}}{4} = \frac{14}{4} = 3.5 \]
  • Hence:
    \[ P = 2(l + w) = 2\left(\frac{14}{5} + 3.5\right) = 2\left(\frac{14}{5} + \frac{7}{2}\right) \]
    \[ = 2\left(\frac{28}{10} + \frac{35}{10}\right) = 2\left(\frac{63}{10}\right) = \frac{126}{10} = 12.6 \]
    Answer: \( P = 12.6 \) meters.
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?
