Line

slides.title: Definition of Line, Segment, and Ray
slides.html:
Knowledge PointLearn the rule

Understand the definition, formula, or theorem before trying a problem.

Core concept

Understand this definition — every example and practice problem in this lesson builds on it.

  • Line: A straight arrangement of points extending infinitely in both directions.
  • Segment: A part of a line with two endpoints.
  • Ray: A part of a line with one endpoint extending infinitely in one direction.
slides.study_tip: Focus on definitions and vocabulary — SAT questions often test whether you recognize the form of an equation.

slides.title: Up next Angle

02Up next

Angle

slides.title: Types of Angles
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

  • Acute Angle: Less than \(90^\circ\).
  • Right Angle: Exactly \(90^\circ\).
  • Obtuse Angle: Greater than \(90^\circ\) but less than \(180^\circ\).
  • Straight Angle: Exactly \(180^\circ\).
  • Reflex Angle: Greater than \(180^\circ\) but less than \(360^\circ\).
  • Complete Angle: Exactly \(360^\circ\).
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Angle Bisector Theorem
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

Statement: If a point lies on the bisector of an angle, then it is equidistant from the two sides of the angle.

Mathematical Representation:

\left\{

\overrightarrow{AD} bisects \angle BAC
DB \perp \overrightarrow{AB}
DC \perp \overrightarrow{AC}

\right\} ⇒ DB = DC
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Exterior Angle Theorem
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

Theorem Statement:

In a triangle, the measure of an exterior angle is equal to the sum of the measures of the two remote interior angles.

Visual Representation:

If \(\displaystyle \angle d\) is the exterior angle , then:

\[ \angle d = \angle a + \angle b \]

SAT Tip:

This theorem is frequently used in problems involving triangles and polygon extensions. It’s a shortcut to avoid summing all three triangle angles when only two are needed!
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Intersecting Lines and Parallel Lines
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

  • Intersecting Lines: Opposite angles are equal. Also, each pair of angles along the same line adds to \(180^\circ\). In the figure above, \( a + b = 180^\circ \).
  • Parallel Lines: Eight angles are formed when a line crosses two parallel lines. The four large angles (\( a \)) are equal, and the four small angles (\( b \)) are equal.
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Question: Finding Angle Measures
slides.html:
Strategy

Mark known angles and side relationships first, then use triangle or parallel-line facts.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

In the figure below, \( \ell \parallel m \), \( r \perp t \), and \( m\angle 1 = 32^\circ \).

Lines \( \ell \), \( r \), and \( t \) intersect at one point.

Find \( m\angle 2 \), \( m\angle 3 \), \( m\angle 4 \), and \( m\angle 5 \).
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Mark known angles and side relationships first, then use triangle or parallel-line facts.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Finding Angle Measures
slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Compare your work with the final answer and fix any missed step.

✦
Your turnWork it out on paper first — reveal when you're ready.

Solution:

  • A right angle measures \( 90^\circ \).
  • Using \( m\angle 1 + m\angle 2 = 90^\circ \):
    \[ 32^\circ + m\angle 2 = 90^\circ \]
    \[ m\angle 2 = 58^\circ \]
  • Vertical angles are congruent:
    \[ m\angle 2 = m\angle 3 = 58^\circ \]
  • A straight angle measures \( 180^\circ \), so:
    \[ m\angle 1 + m\angle 4 + m\angle 3 = 180^\circ \]
    \[ 32^\circ + m\angle 4 + 58^\circ = 180^\circ \]
    \[ m\angle 4 = 90^\circ \]
  • Alternate interior angles are congruent:
    \[ m\angle 3 = m\angle 5 = 58^\circ \]
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Finding Angle Measures
slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Compare your work with the final answer and fix any missed step.

  • Corresponding angles are congruent:
    \[ m\angle 1 = m\angle 6 = 32^\circ \]
    Final Answers:
    \[ m\angle 2 = 58^\circ, m\angle 3 = 58^\circ, m\angle 4 = 90^\circ, m\angle 5 = 58^\circ. \]
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question: Finding TUR
slides.html:
Strategy

Mark known angles and side relationships first, then use triangle or parallel-line facts.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

In the figure shown, points \( Q, R, S, \) and \( T \) lie on line segment \( PV \),

and line segment \( RU \) intersects line segment \( SX \) at point \( W \).

Given:

  • \( \angle SQX = 48^\circ \)
  • \( \angle SXQ = 86^\circ \)
  • \( \angle SWU = 85^\circ \)
  • \( \angle VTU = 162^\circ \)

What is the measure, in degrees, of \( \angle TUR \)?

Your answer

Use ^ for exponents and / for fractions.
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Mark known angles and side relationships first, then use triangle or parallel-line facts.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Finding TUR
slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Compare your work with the final answer and fix any missed step.

✦
Your turnWork it out on paper first — reveal when you're ready.

Solution:

  • Compute \( \angle XQS \) using the given angles:
    \[ \angle XQS = 180^\circ - \angle SXQ = 180^\circ - 86^\circ = 94^\circ \]
  • Compute \( \angle XSW \) using \( \angle SQX \) and \( \angle XQS \):
    \[ \angle XSW = 180^\circ - (\angle SQX + \angle XQS) = 180^\circ - (48^\circ + 94^\circ) = 38^\circ \]
  • Use \( \angle SWU = 85^\circ \) to find \( \angle WUR \):
    \[ \angle WUR = 180^\circ - (\angle SWU + \angle XSW) = 180^\circ - (85^\circ + 38^\circ) = 57^\circ \]
  • Compute \( \angle TUR \) using \( \angle VTU = 162^\circ \):
    \[ \angle TUR = 180^\circ - \angle WUR = 180^\circ - 57^\circ = 123^\circ \]
Final Answer
\(\boxed{123^\circ} \).\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Polygons and Interior Angles
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

Polygon Angle Sum Formula:

\[ \text{Sum of interior angles} = 180^\circ(n - 2) \]
  • \(\displaystyle n\) = number of sides
  • Each additional side increases the angle sum by \(\displaystyle 180^\circ\)

Regular Polygon:

All sides and angles are equal.

Measure of Each Interior Angle in a Regular Polygon:

\[ \text{Each angle} = \frac{180(n - 2)}{n} \]
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Question: Polygon Angle Problem
slides.html:
Strategy

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

A regular polygon has an interior angle that is 4 times the exterior plus 60 the measure of its exterior angle.

Question:

How many sides does the polygon have?

Choices:

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Mark known angles and side relationships first, then use triangle or parallel-line facts.

slides.title: Answer and Explanation
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

"A regular polygon has an interior angle that is 60 more than four times its exterior angle."

Then:

\[ x = 4y + 60, x + y = 180 ⇒ 4y + 60 + y = 180 \]

✅ Correct Answer: D. 15

03Up next

Triangle

slides.title: Isosceles and Equilateral Triangles
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

Isosceles Triangle:

  • Has two equal sides.
  • The angles opposite the equal sides are also equal.
  • Often appears in SAT triangle congruence or symmetry problems.

Equilateral Triangle:

  • All sides are equal.
  • Each angle measures \(\displaystyle 60^\circ\).
  • Always a regular polygon.
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Question: Area of Equilateral Triangles
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify the formula, then track how scaling or unit changes affect the result.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

A graphic designer is creating a logo for a company. The logo is shown in the figure above. The logo is in the shape of a trapezoid and consists of three congruent equilateral triangles.

If the perimeter of the logo is 20 centimeters, what is the combined area of the shaded regions, in square centimeters, of the logo?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify the formula, then track how scaling or unit changes affect the result.

slides.title: Answer: Area of Equilateral Triangles in a Logo
slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Compare your work with the final answer and fix any missed step.

In the diagram, the trapezoid has 4 edges, and each triangle has 3 sides. The full figure has:

\[ \text{Perimeter} = 5s ⇒ 5s = 20 ⇒ s = 4 \]

Area of one triangle:

\[ A = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}(4)^2 = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}(16) = 4\sqrt{3} \]

Two shaded triangles:

\[ 2 \cdot 4\sqrt{3} = 8\sqrt{3} \]
Correct Answer
C \(8\sqrt{3}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Right Triangles
slides.html:
Knowledge PointLearn the rule

Understand the definition, formula, or theorem before trying a problem.

Core concept

Understand this definition — every example and practice problem in this lesson builds on it.

Definition: A triangle with one \(\displaystyle 90^\circ\) angle.

Pythagorean Theorem:

\[ a^2 + b^2 = c^2 \]

Where \(\displaystyle a\) and \(\displaystyle b\) are legs and \(\displaystyle c\) is the hypotenuse.

Pythagorean Triples (SAT favorites):

  • \(\displaystyle (3, 4, 5)\)
  • \(\displaystyle (5, 12, 13)\)
slides.study_tip: Focus on definitions and vocabulary — SAT questions often test whether you recognize the form of an equation.

slides.title: Special Right Triangles
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

1. 45°-45°-90° Triangle

  • Legs are equal.
  • Hypotenuse: \(\displaystyle x\sqrt{2}\) if each leg is \(\displaystyle x\).

2. 30°-60°-90° Triangle

  • Short leg: \(\displaystyle x\)
  • Long leg: \(\displaystyle x\sqrt{3}\)
  • Hypotenuse: \(\displaystyle 2x\)
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Question: Geometry – Right Triangle with Altitude
slides.html:
Strategy

Mark known angles and side relationships first, then use triangle or parallel-line facts.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

In triangle \( \triangle ABC \), the measure of angle \( B \) is \( 90^\circ \), and \( \overline{BD} \) is an altitude of the triangle.

  • The length of \( \overline{AB} \) is \( 15 \),
  • The length of \( \overline{AC} \) is \( 23 \) greater than the length of \( \overline{AB} \),

What is the value of \( \dfrac{BC}{BD} \)?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Mark known angles and side relationships first, then use triangle or parallel-line facts.

slides.title: Answer: Geometry – Right Triangle
slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Compare your work with the final answer and fix any missed step.

We are given:

  • \( AB = 15 \)
  • \( AC = AB + 23 = 15 + 23 = 38 \)
  • \( \angle B = 90^\circ \), so triangle \( \triangle ABC \) is a right triangle.

Using the Pythagorean Theorem:

\[ AB^2 + BC^2 = AC^2 ⇒ 15^2 + BC^2 = 38^2 \]
Correct Answer
D \(\dfrac{38}{15}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Congruent Triangles: Definition and Proof
slides.html:
Knowledge PointLearn the rule

Understand the definition, formula, or theorem before trying a problem.

Core concept

Understand this definition — every example and practice problem in this lesson builds on it.

Definition:

Two triangles are congruent if all corresponding sides and angles are equal. That means:

\[ \text{Side} = \text{Side}, \text{Angle} = \text{Angle} \]
\[ \triangle ABC \cong \triangle DEF ⇒ AB = DE,\ \angle A = \angle D,\ \text{etc.} \]

Methods to Prove Triangle Congruence:

  • SSS (Side-Side-Side): All three sides of one triangle are equal to the corresponding sides of another.
  • SAS (Side-Angle-Side): Two sides and the included angle are congruent.
  • ASA (Angle-Side-Angle): Two angles and the included side are congruent.
  • HL (Hypotenuse-Leg): In right triangles, hypotenuse and one leg are congruent.

SAT Tip:

Do not assume triangle congruence from AAA or SSA — these are not valid for proving congruence.
slides.study_tip: Focus on definitions and vocabulary — SAT questions often test whether you recognize the form of an equation.

slides.title: Question: Triangle Congruence
slides.html:
Strategy

Mark known angles and side relationships first, then use triangle or parallel-line facts.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

In triangles \( ABC \) and \( DEF \), angles \( B \) and \( E \) each have measure \( 27^\circ \), and angles \( C \) and \( F \) each have measure \( 41^\circ \).

Which additional piece of information is sufficient to determine whether triangle \( ABC \) is congruent to triangle \( DEF \)?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Mark known angles and side relationships first, then use triangle or parallel-line facts.

slides.title: Answer: Triangle Congruence
slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Compare your work with the final answer and fix any missed step.

Since two angles are known in each triangle:

\[ \angle B = \angle E = 27^\circ, \angle C = \angle F = 41^\circ \]

We can determine the third angle because the angles of a triangle sum to \(180^\circ\):

\[ \angle A = \angle D = 180^\circ - 27^\circ - 41^\circ = 112^\circ \]

Therefore, all three angles in both triangles are known and equal:

\[ \angle A = \angle D, \angle B = \angle E, \angle C = \angle F \]

Thus, the two triangles are similar (AAA), but to determine congruence, one side must be known.

If one side length (e.g., \( AB \)) is known, the Side-Angle-Angle (ASA) or Angle-Side-Angle (ASA) congruence criterion can be applied.

Answer
C
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Similar Triangles
slides.html:
Knowledge PointLearn the rule

Understand the definition, formula, or theorem before trying a problem.

Core concept

Understand this definition — every example and practice problem in this lesson builds on it.

Definition:

Triangles are similar if their corresponding angles are equal and corresponding sides are in proportion.

Symbolically: \(\displaystyle \triangle BAC \sim \triangle BDE\)

Properties:

  • All corresponding angles are equal.
  • All corresponding sides are proportional.
  • \(\displaystyle \frac{DB}{AB} = \frac{BE}{BC} = \frac{DE}{AC}\)

SAT Tip:

Use proportions to solve for missing side lengths or establish ratios between parts of a figure.
slides.study_tip: Focus on definitions and vocabulary — SAT questions often test whether you recognize the form of an equation.

slides.title: Similar Triangles: Definition and Proof Methods
slides.html:
Knowledge PointLearn the rule

Understand the definition, formula, or theorem before trying a problem.

Core concept

Understand this definition — every example and practice problem in this lesson builds on it.

Definition:

Two triangles are similar if their corresponding angles are equal and their corresponding sides are in proportion.

\[ \triangle ABC \sim \triangle DEF ⇒ \angle A = \angle D,\ \angle B = \angle E,\ \angle C = \angle F \]
\[ \frac{AB}{DE} = \frac{BC}{EF} = \frac{AC}{DF} \]

Ways to Prove Similar Triangles:

  • AA (Angle-Angle): If two angles of one triangle are equal to two angles of another, the triangles are similar.
  • SSS (Side-Side-Side) Similarity: If all three pairs of corresponding sides are in proportion.
  • SAS (Side-Angle-Side) Similarity: If two sides are in proportion and the included angle is equal.
slides.study_tip: Focus on definitions and vocabulary — SAT questions often test whether you recognize the form of an equation.

slides.title: Similar Triangles in Right Triangle Model
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

Concept:

In a right triangle, when an altitude is drawn from the right angle to the hypotenuse, it creates two smaller triangles that are similar to the original triangle and to each other.

Proportional Relationship:

\[ \frac{a}{h} = \frac{h}{b} ⇒ h = \sqrt{ab} \]

where:

  • \(\displaystyle a\) and \(\displaystyle b\) are the segments of the hypotenuse,
  • \(\displaystyle h\) is the altitude to the hypotenuse.

Tip:

This model leads to fast solutions when given two sides and asked for height or area.
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Question: Triangle Area and Geometry Constraints
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify the formula, then track how scaling or unit changes affect the result.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

Given:

  • The area of triangle \( ABC \) is at least 48 but no more than 60.
  • \( y \) is an integer.

What is one possible value of \( x \)?

Your answer

Enter an exact fraction or decimal equivalent.
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify the formula, then track how scaling or unit changes affect the result.

slides.title: Answer: Triangle Area and Geometry Constraints
slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Compare your work with the final answer and fix any missed step.

We are given:

\[ \text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \times \text{base} \times \text{height} = \frac{1}{2} \times 12 \times y = 6y \]
\[ 48 \leq 6y \leq 60 ⇒ 8 \leq y \leq 10 \]

So possible integer values for \( y \) are \( 8, 9, \) or \( 10 \).

We are told that \( x \) is the distance from point \( A \) to where the perpendicular from \( B \) hits base \( AC \), which lies between \( A \) and \( C \). Since \( AC = 12 \), one possible value of \( x \) that lies between 0 and 12 is:

\[ \boxed{\frac{25}{6}} \]

Possible Answer: 5
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question: Right Triangle Geometry
slides.html:
Strategy

Mark known angles and side relationships first, then use triangle or parallel-line facts.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

In the figure above, what is the length of segment \( \overline{NQ} \)?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Mark known angles and side relationships first, then use triangle or parallel-line facts.

slides.title: Answer: Right Triangle Geometry
slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Compare your work with the final answer and fix any missed step.

Use the full triangle \( \triangle MNP \) with legs \( MN = 3 \) and \( NP = 4 \). That makes \( MP \) the hypotenuse:

\[ MP^2 = MN^2 + NP^2 = 3^2 + 4^2 = 9 + 16 = 25 ⇒ MP = 5 \]

So triangle \( \triangle MNP \) is a right triangle with hypotenuse 5. Since \( \angle MQN \) is a right angle, \( NQ \) is the height to the hypotenuse.

Area of triangle using base and height:

\[ \text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot MP \cdot NQ = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 5 \cdot NQ \]

Area using two legs:

\[ \text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 3 \cdot 4 = 6 \]

Equating both:

\[ \frac{1}{2} \cdot 5 \cdot NQ = 6 ⇒ \frac{5}{2} \cdot NQ = 6 ⇒ NQ = \frac{12}{5} = 2.4 \]
Correct Answer
C 2.4
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Area of an Isosceles Right Triangle
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify the formula, then track how scaling or unit changes affect the result.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

Question:

The hypotenuse of an isosceles right triangle has a length of \( 12 \) units. What is the area of the triangle, in square units?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify the formula, then track how scaling or unit changes affect the result.

slides.title: Solution
slides.html:
Solution ReviewStudy the reasoning

Read the steps carefully, then summarize the method in your own words.

✦
Your turnWork it out on paper first — reveal when you're ready.

Solution:

  • Let the length of each leg of the isosceles right triangle be \( x \).
  • Then:
    \[ x^2 + x^2 = 12^2 \]
    \[ 2x^2 = 144 \]
    \[ x^2 = 72 \]
    \[ x = 6\sqrt{2} \]
  • Therefore, the area is:
    \[ A = \frac{1}{2} \times x \times x = \frac{1}{2} \times (6\sqrt{2}) \times (6\sqrt{2}) = \frac{1}{2} \times 72 = 36 \]
Answer
\(\boxed{36}\)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.html:
Solution ReviewStudy the reasoning

Read the steps carefully, then summarize the method in your own words.

✦
Your turnWork it out on paper first — reveal when you're ready.

Solution:

To determine whether two triangles are similar, we need:

  • Two pairs of corresponding angles to be congruent (AA~criterion).
  • Alternatively, corresponding sides to be proportional (SSS~or~SAS~criterion).

Given \( \angle L = \angle R = 127^\circ \):

  • Knowing either \( \angle N \) and \( \angle T \) or \( \angle S \) and \( \angle T \) would be sufficient by AA criterion.

Therefore:

\[ \boxed{\text{Option A: The measures of angles } N \text{ and } T} \]

Trigonometric Ratios of Acute Angles

slides.title: Trigonometric Ratios and Cofunctions
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

Trigonometric Ratios:

A ratio of the lengths of sides of a right triangle.

\[ \sin\theta = \frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{hypotenuse}} = \frac{a}{c}, \cos\theta = \frac{\text{adjacent}}{\text{hypotenuse}} = \frac{b}{c}, \tan\theta = \frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{adjacent}} = \frac{a}{b} \]

Cofunction Identity:

In a right triangle \(\displaystyle ABC\), angles \(\displaystyle A\) and \(\displaystyle B\) are complementary:

\[ m\angle A + m\angle B = 90^\circ \]

So:

\[ \sin(\theta) = \cos(90^\circ - \theta), \tan(\theta) = \cot(90^\circ - \theta) \]
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Trigonometric Ratios Are Constant Ratios
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

Key Concept:

Trigonometric functions like \(\displaystyle \sin \theta\), \(\displaystyle \cos \theta\), and \(\displaystyle \tan \theta\) represent ratios of side lengths in a right triangle.

Important Insight:

For any given angle \(\displaystyle \theta\), the ratio is always the same — no matter the size of the triangle — as long as it's a right triangle.

Examples:

  • \(\displaystyle \sin(30^\circ) = \dfrac{1}{2}\) — In every right triangle with a \(\displaystyle 30^\circ\) angle, the opposite side is half the hypotenuse.
  • \(\displaystyle \cos(60^\circ) = \dfrac{1}{2}\) — In any right triangle with a \(\displaystyle 60^\circ\) angle, the adjacent side is half the hypotenuse.
  • \(\displaystyle \tan(45^\circ) = 1\) — In any right triangle with a \(\displaystyle 45^\circ\) angle, the opposite and adjacent sides are equal.

SAT Tip:

Memorize the sine, cosine, and tangent of special angles: \(\displaystyle 30^\circ\), \(\displaystyle 45^\circ\), and \(\displaystyle 60^\circ\) — they appear frequently on the test!
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Question
slides.html:

\[\begin{aligned} RS &= 20 \\ ST &= 48 \\ TR &= 52 \end{aligned}\]

The side lengths of right triangle \(\displaystyle RST\) are given. Triangle \(\displaystyle RST\) is similar to triangle \(\displaystyle UVW\), where \(\displaystyle S\) corresponds to \(\displaystyle V\) and \(\displaystyle T\) corresponds to \(\displaystyle W\). What is the value of \(\displaystyle \tan W\)?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Mark known angles and side relationships first, then use triangle or parallel-line facts.

slides.title: Answer
slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Compare your work with the final answer and fix any missed step.

Since triangle \(\displaystyle RST\) is a right triangle with hypotenuse \(\displaystyle TR = 52\), and legs \(\displaystyle RS = 20\), \(\displaystyle ST = 48\), the angle at \(\displaystyle W\) corresponds to angle \(\displaystyle T\).

\[ \tan(W) = \frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{adjacent}} = \frac{RS}{ST} = \frac{20}{48} = \frac{5}{12} \]
\[ \boxed{\text{Answer: B. } \frac{5}{12}} \]

Radian Measure

slides.title: Understanding Radian Measure
slides.html:
Knowledge PointLearn the rule

Understand the definition, formula, or theorem before trying a problem.

Core concept

Understand this definition — every example and practice problem in this lesson builds on it.

Definition of Radian:

A radian is the angle created when the arc length equals the radius.

\[ 1 \text{ radian} = \frac{s}{r} \]
  • \(\displaystyle s\): Arc length
  • \(\displaystyle r\): Radius

Important Note:

There are \(\displaystyle 2\pi\) radians in a full circle

Degree to Radian Conversion:

\[ \theta\ (\text{in radians}) = \theta^\circ \cdot \frac{\pi}{180} \]

Radian Conversion to Degree to Conversion:

\[ \theta\ (\text{in degree}) = \theta \cdot \frac{180}{\pi} \]
slides.study_tip: Focus on definitions and vocabulary — SAT questions often test whether you recognize the form of an equation.

slides.title: What If the Angle is Greater Than 90 Degrees?
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

Reference Angle:

The reference angle is the acute angle (less than \(90^\circ\)) formed between the terminal side of an angle in standard position and the \(x\)-axis.

Why Use It?

  • Trig values for any angle \(\theta\) (in degrees or radians) can be found by using its reference angle.
  • The sine, cosine, and tangent of the original angle have the same absolute value as the reference angle — with signs determined by the quadrant.

How to Find the Reference Angle:
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: What If the Angle is Greater Than 90 Degrees?
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

Big idea: for angles outside a right triangle, do not start by drawing a new triangle.

Start with the angle's position on the coordinate plane.

Use this 4-step routine:

  1. Draw the angle in standard position: start on the positive \(x\)-axis and rotate counterclockwise.
  2. Identify the quadrant where the terminal side lands.
  3. Find the reference angle: the acute angle between the terminal side and the \(x\)-axis.
  4. Use the reference-angle value, then attach the correct sign.
QuadrantPositive trig ratio
I\sin,\ \cos,\ \tan
II\sin
III\tan
IV\cos
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Trigonometric Functions on the Unit Circle
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

What the unit circle gives us:

Put angle \(\theta\) in standard position and look at the point where the terminal side hits the circle.

If that point is \((x,y)\), then:

\[ \cos(\theta)=x,\qquad \sin(\theta)=y,\qquad \tan(\theta)=\frac{y}{x} (x\ne 0) \]

So when solving a unit-circle problem:

  1. Reduce the angle to a familiar terminal position.
  2. Find the point \((x,y)\) on the unit circle.
  3. Read \(\sin\) from \(y\), read \(\cos\) from \(x\), and use \(y/x\) for \(\tan\).
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Question: Trigonometric Constant Expression
slides.html:
Strategy

Label opposite, adjacent, and hypotenuse relative to the given angle.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

The value of \( \tan\left( \frac{49\pi}{6} \right) \) can be written as \( k\sqrt{3} \), where \( k \) is a constant.

What is the value of \( k \)?

Choices:

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Label opposite, adjacent, and hypotenuse relative to the given angle.

slides.title: Answer Explanation: Trigonometric Constant Expression
slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Compare your work with the final answer and fix any missed step.

To evaluate \( \tan\left( \frac{49\pi}{6} \right) \), reduce the angle:

\[ \frac{49\pi}{6} = 8\pi + \frac{\pi}{6} \]

Since \( 2\pi \) is one full rotation, this is coterminal with \( \frac{\pi}{6} \).

So,

\[ \tan\left( \frac{49\pi}{6} \right) = \tan\left( \frac{\pi}{6} \right) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \]

Thus, we write:

\[ \tan\left( \frac{49\pi}{6} \right) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} = k\sqrt{3} ⇒ k = \frac{1}{3} \]

Correct Answer: A. \( \boxed{\frac{1}{3}} \)
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Answer to Unit Circle Problem
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

Since the angle is \( 9\pi \), which is equivalent to \( \pi \) (modulo \( 2\pi \)), the terminal point is the same as \( \pi \) on the unit circle.

\[ \cos(\pi) = -1, \sin(\pi) = 0 ⇒ M = (-1, 0) \]

But based on options that suggest a reference angle of \( \frac{\pi}{6} \), observe:

\[ \frac{27\pi}{3} = 9\pi = 4(2\pi) + \pi ⇒ \text{same terminal side as } \pi \]

This implies a point with coordinate:

\[ a = -1, \text{ not among options.} \]

However, if the actual question is about angle \( \frac{2\pi}{3} \) (possibly a typo), then:

\[ \sin\left(\frac{2\pi}{3}\right) = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \]

Correct Answer: B.

slides.title: Trigonometry
slides.html:
Strategy

Mark known angles and side relationships first, then use triangle or parallel-line facts.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

Question:

Triangle \(JKL\) is similar to triangle \(RST\), where angle \(J\) corresponds to angle \(R\) and angles \(K\) and \(S\) are right angles. If \(\cos(L) = \frac{119}{169}\), what is the value of \(\tan(T)\)?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Mark known angles and side relationships first, then use triangle or parallel-line facts.

slides.title: Solution
slides.html:
Solution ReviewStudy the reasoning

Read the steps carefully, then summarize the method in your own words.

✦
Your turnWork it out on paper first — reveal when you're ready.

Solution:

Since the triangles \(JKL\) and \(RST\) are similar, corresponding angles are congruent, so \(L\) corresponds to \(T\).

Given:

\[ \cos(L) = \frac{119}{169} \]

Using the Pythagorean identity:

\[ \sin^2(L) + \cos^2(L) = 1 ⇒ \sin(L) = \sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{119}{169}\right)^2} \]
\[ \tan(L) = \frac{\sin(L)}{\cos(L)} = \frac{\sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{119}{169}\right)^2}}{\frac{119}{169}} = \frac{\sqrt{169^2 - 119^2}}{119} \]
\[ 169^2 - 119^2 = (169 - 119)(169 + 119) = 50 \times 288 = 14400 \]
\[ \sqrt{14400} = 120 \]

So:

\[ \tan(T) = \tan(L) = \frac{120}{119} \]
\[ \boxed{\text{Option C: } \frac{120}{119}} \]
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Fraction of Circumference
slides.html:
Strategy

Rewrite the equation in standard form to read off the center and radius.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

Question:

Point \( O \) is the center of a circle. The measure of arc \( MN \) on this circle is \(\frac{4\pi}{3}\) radians. The length of arc \( MN \) is what fraction of the circumference of the circle?

Your answer

Enter an exact fraction or decimal equivalent.
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Rewrite the equation in standard form to read off the center and radius.

slides.title: Solution
slides.html:
Solution ReviewStudy the reasoning

Read the steps carefully, then summarize the method in your own words.

✦
Your turnWork it out on paper first — reveal when you're ready.

Solution:

  • Recall that the full circumference of a circle is \(2\pi\) radians in angle measure (equivalent to \(360^\circ\)).
  • The measure of arc \( MN \) is given as \(\frac{4\pi}{3}\) radians.
  • To find the fraction of the circumference represented by this arc:
\[ \text{Fraction} = \frac{\frac{4\pi}{3}}{2\pi} = \frac{4}{6} = \frac{2}{3} \]
\[ \boxed{\frac{2}{3}} \]
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Acute Angle with the x-axis
slides.html:
Strategy

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

Question:

In the \( xy \)-plane, segment \( OA \) has endpoints \( (0,0) \) and \( (\sqrt{3}, \sqrt{3}) \). What is the measure, in radians, of the acute angle formed by segment \( OA \) and the line \( y = 0 \)?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Mark known angles and side relationships first, then use triangle or parallel-line facts.

slides.title: Solution
slides.html:
Solution ReviewStudy the reasoning

Read the steps carefully, then summarize the method in your own words.

✦
Your turnWork it out on paper first — reveal when you're ready.

Solution:

  • The segment \( OA \) has a slope:
\[ \tan(\theta) = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{3}} = 1 \]
  • Therefore:
\[ \theta = \arctan(1) = \frac{\pi}{4} \]
  • Thus:
\[ \boxed{\frac{\pi}{4}} \]
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

Arc Length and Area of a Sector
slides.section: Arc Length and Area of a Sector

slides.title: Arc Length of a Circle
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

Arc Length Formula:

\[ s = r\theta \]

(where \(\displaystyle s\) = arc length, \(\displaystyle r\) = radius, \(\displaystyle \theta\) = central angle in radians)

Example:

Find the arc length of a circle with:

  • Radius: \(\displaystyle r = 6\)
  • Central angle: \(\displaystyle \theta = 60^\circ = \frac{\pi}{3}\) radians
\[ s = r\theta = 6 \cdot \frac{\pi}{3} = 2\pi \]

SAT Tip:

Do not use this formula unless \(\displaystyle \theta\) is in radians!
slides.section: Arc Length and Area of a Sector
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Area of a Sector
slides.html:
Knowledge PointLearn the rule

Understand the definition, formula, or theorem before trying a problem.

Core concept

Understand this definition — every example and practice problem in this lesson builds on it.

Definition:

A sector is a "pizza slice" portion of a circle.

Formula (Radians):

\[ \text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} r^2 \theta \]

Formula (Degrees):

\[ \text{Area} = \frac{\theta^\circ}{360^\circ} \cdot \pi r^2 \]

Example:

Find the area of a sector of a circle with radius 4 and angle \(\displaystyle 45^\circ\):

\[ \text{Area} = \frac{45}{360} \cdot \pi \cdot 4^2 = \frac{1}{8} \cdot 16\pi = 2\pi \]

SAT Reminder: Be sure whether angle is in radians or degrees before using a formula.
slides.section: Arc Length and Area of a Sector
slides.study_tip: Focus on definitions and vocabulary — SAT questions often test whether you recognize the form of an equation.

slides.title: Question: Arc Length and Central Angle
slides.html:
Strategy

Mark known angles and side relationships first, then use triangle or parallel-line facts.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

The circle above has center \( O \), the length of arc \( \widehat{ADC} \) is \( 5\pi \), and \( x = 100 \).

What is the length of arc \( \widehat{ABC} \)?

Choose your answer
slides.section: Arc Length and Area of a Sector
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Mark known angles and side relationships first, then use triangle or parallel-line facts.

slides.title: Answer: Arc Length and Central Angle
slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Compare your work with the final answer and fix any missed step.

We are told:

  • \( \text{Length of arc } \widehat{ADC} = 5\pi \)
  • Central angle \( \angle AOC = x = 100^\circ \)

Since the entire circle represents \( 360^\circ \), the arc \( \widehat{ABC} \) must span:

\[ 360^\circ - 100^\circ = 260^\circ \]

Using the proportion of arc to full circle:

\[ \frac{260}{100} = \frac{\text{Length of } \widehat{ABC}}{\text{Length of } \widehat{ADC}} = \frac{L}{5\pi} ⇒ L = 5\pi \cdot \frac{260}{100} = 13\pi \]
Correct Answer
B \(13\pi\)

slides.title: Up next Properties of the Circle

02Up next

Properties of the Circle

slides.title: Angles Inscribed in a Semicircle
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

Theorem:

Any angle inscribed in a semicircle is a right angle (90°).

  • If a triangle is inscribed in a circle and one side is the diameter, then the angle opposite that side is \(\displaystyle 90^\circ\).

Example:

If triangle \(\displaystyle ACB\) is inscribed in a circle and \(\displaystyle AB\) is a diameter, then:

\[ \angle C = 90^\circ \]

SAT Tip:

Look for a triangle with one side as a diameter — it’s a right triangle!
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Radius and Tangent Line
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

Key Property:

The radius of a circle is perpendicular to the tangent line at the point of contact.

  • Use this right angle in triangle questions.
  • Often used in slope problems — if radius has slope \(\displaystyle m\), tangent has slope \(\displaystyle -\frac{1}{m}\).

SAT Tip:

If the question mentions “tangent”, check for perpendicularity with radius.
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Radius Bisecting a Chord
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

Theorem:

If a radius (or diameter) is perpendicular to a chord, it bisects the chord.

\[ \text{If } AB \perp OE,\ \text{then } AD = DB \]
  • Can be used to find missing lengths using right triangle properties.
  • Also useful for coordinate geometry questions involving symmetry.

Note: The converse is also true — if a radius bisects a chord, it’s perpendicular.

Useful for: Midpoint formula, Pythagorean theorem, and algebraic proofs.
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Question: Arc Length in Circle Geometry
slides.html:
Strategy

Mark known angles and side relationships first, then use triangle or parallel-line facts.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

Point \( O \) is the center of the circle above, and the measure of \( \angle OAB \) is \( 30^\circ \).

If the length of \( \overline{OC} \) is \( 18 \), what is the length of arc \( \widehat{AB} \)?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Mark known angles and side relationships first, then use triangle or parallel-line facts.

slides.title: Answer: Arc Length in Circle Geometry
slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Compare your work with the final answer and fix any missed step.

Since \( \angle OAB = 30^\circ \), the central angle for arc \( AB \) is \( 60^\circ \) (as it spans angle \( AOB \)).

The radius is \( r = 18 \), so the circumference of the full circle is:

\[ C = 2\pi r = 2\pi(18) = 36\pi. \]

The arc \( \widehat{AB} \) represents \( \frac{120^\circ}{360^\circ} = \frac{1}{3} \) of the full circle, so its length is:

\[ \frac{1}{3} \times 36\pi = 12\pi. \]
Correct Answer
B \(12\pi\)

Equation of a Circle in XY-Plane
slides.section: Equation of a Circle in XY-Plane

slides.title: Equation of a Circle (Coordinate Plane)
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

Standard Form:

\[ (x - h)^2 + (y - k)^2 = r^2 \]

Where:

  • \(\displaystyle (h, k)\) is the center,
  • \(\displaystyle r\) is the radius.

Example:

Circle with center \(\displaystyle (3, -2)\) and radius \(\displaystyle 5\):

\[ (x - 3)^2 + (y + 2)^2 = 25 \]

SAT Skills:

  • Completing the square to rewrite equations.
  • Identify center and radius from expanded forms.
slides.section: Equation of a Circle in XY-Plane
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Completing the Square to Find Circle Equation
slides.html:
Knowledge PointBuild the method

Focus on the worked idea and the steps you will reuse later.

Given:

\[ x^2 + y^2 - 6x + 8y + 9 = 0 \]

Steps:

  1. Group \(\displaystyle x\) and \(\displaystyle y\) terms:
    \[ (x^2 - 6x) + (y^2 + 8y) = -9 \]
  2. Complete the square:
    \[ (x - 3)^2 - 9 + (y + 4)^2 - 16 = -9 \]
  3. Simplify:
    \[ (x - 3)^2 + (y + 4)^2 = 16 \]
Final Answer
C \(enter = \((3, -4)\), Radius = \(4\)\)

SAT Tip: Always isolate constants and complete square carefully on both \(\displaystyle x\) and \(\displaystyle y\) terms.
slides.section: Equation of a Circle in XY-Plane
slides.study_tip: Take notes on anything that feels new — you'll reuse these ideas in practice sets.

slides.title: Question: Completing the Square
slides.html:
Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

The graph of the equation

\[ x^2 + x + y^2 + y = \frac{199}{2} \]

in the \(xy\)-plane is a circle. What is the length of the circle’s radius?
slides.section: Equation of a Circle in XY-Plane
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Rewrite the equation in standard form to read off the center and radius.

slides.title: Answer: Completing the Square – Radius of a Circle
slides.html:
Answer ReviewCheck and correct

Compare your work with the final answer and fix any missed step.

To convert the given equation to the standard form of a circle, complete the square for both \(x\) and \(y\):

\[ x^2 + x + y^2 + y = \frac{199}{2} \]

Complete the square:

\[ x^2 + x = \left(x + \frac{1}{2}\right)^2 - \frac{1}{4}, y^2 + y = \left(y + \frac{1}{2}\right)^2 - \frac{1}{4} \]

So,

\[ \left(x + \frac{1}{2}\right)^2 - \frac{1}{4} + \left(y + \frac{1}{2}\right)^2 - \frac{1}{4} = \frac{199}{2} \]
\[ \left(x + \frac{1}{2}\right)^2 + \left(y + \frac{1}{2}\right)^2 = \frac{199}{2} + \frac{1}{2} = \frac{200}{2} = 100 \]

Thus, the radius is:

\[ r = \sqrt{100} = 10 \]
slides.section: Equation of a Circle in XY-Plane
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Equation of a Circle
slides.html:
Strategy

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

Question:

The center of a circle in the \(xy\)-plane is at \((1, -1)\) and the point \((-5, 7)\) lies on the circle. Which of the following equations represents this circle?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: For multiple choice, eliminate obviously wrong options before doing full algebra.
slides.strategy_hint: Rewrite the equation in standard form to read off the center and radius.

slides.title: Solution
slides.html:
Solution ReviewStudy the reasoning

Read the steps carefully, then summarize the method in your own words.

✦
Your turnWork it out on paper first — reveal when you're ready.

Solution:

  • The center is at \((1, -1)\).
  • Find the radius using the distance formula:
\[ r = \sqrt{(-5 - 1)^2 + (7 - (-1))^2} = \sqrt{(-6)^2 + (8)^2} = \sqrt{36 + 64} = \sqrt{100} = 10 \]
  • The circle’s equation is:
\[ (x - 1)^2 + (y + 1)^2 = 100 \]
\[ \boxed{\text{Answer: D}} \]
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Circles in the xy-Plane
slides.html:
Strategy

Label opposite, adjacent, and hypotenuse relative to the given angle.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

Question:

In the \(xy\)-plane, circle \(A\) is defined by the equation \((x - 3)^2 + (y - 4)^2 = 16\), and circle \(B\) is defined by the equation \((x - 3)^2 + (y - 4)^2 = n\), where \(n\) is a constant. The diameter of circle \(B\) is 48 units longer than the diameter of circle \(A\). What is the value of \(n\)?

Your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Label opposite, adjacent, and hypotenuse relative to the given angle.

slides.title: Solution
slides.html:
Solution ReviewStudy the reasoning

Read the steps carefully, then summarize the method in your own words.

✦
Your turnWork it out on paper first — reveal when you're ready.

Solution:

  • Circle \(A\):
    \[ r_A = \sqrt{16} = 4 ⇒ d_A = 2 \times 4 = 8 \]
  • Circle \(B\):
    \[ d_B = d_A + 48 = 8 + 48 = 56 ⇒ r_B = 28 \]
  • Therefore:
    \[ r_B^2 = 28^2 = 784 \]
    Hence:
    \[ \boxed{n = 784} \]
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Circle in the xy-Plane
slides.html:
Strategy

Rewrite the equation in standard form to read off the center and radius.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

Question:

The given equation represents a circle in the \(xy\)-plane:

\[ x^2 + y^2 + 7x + y = -\frac{7}{2} \]

What is the length of the radius of the circle?

Your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Rewrite the equation in standard form to read off the center and radius.

slides.title: Solution
slides.html:
Solution ReviewStudy the reasoning

Read the steps carefully, then summarize the method in your own words.

  • Start with:
    \[ x^2 + 7x + y^2 + y = -\frac{7}{2} \]
  • Complete the square:
    \[ x^2 + 7x \rightarrow x^2 + 7x + \left(\frac{7}{2}\right)^2 = \left(x + \frac{7}{2}\right)^2 - \frac{49}{4} \]
    \[ y^2 + y \rightarrow y^2 + y + \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^2 = \left(y + \frac{1}{2}\right)^2 - \frac{1}{4} \]
  • Combine:
    \[ \left(x + \frac{7}{2}\right)^2 - \frac{49}{4} + \left(y + \frac{1}{2}\right)^2 - \frac{1}{4} = -\frac{7}{2} \]
  • Bring constants to the right:
    \[ \left(x + \frac{7}{2}\right)^2 + \left(y + \frac{1}{2}\right)^2 = -\frac{7}{2} + \frac{49}{4} + \frac{1}{4} \]
    \[ = -\frac{7}{2} + \frac{50}{4} \]
    \[ = -\frac{14}{4} + \frac{50}{4} = \frac{36}{4} = 9 \]
  • Therefore:
    \[ r = \sqrt{9} = \boxed{3} \]
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Tangent Line and Circle
slides.html:
Strategy

Label opposite, adjacent, and hypotenuse relative to the given angle.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

Question:

A circle in the \(xy\)-plane has the equation

\[ (x-2)^2 + (y-2)^2 = 25. \]

Line \(m\) is tangent to this circle at the point \((5,6)\). What is the \(y\)-coordinate of the \(y\)-intercept of line \(m\)?

Your answer

Enter an exact fraction or decimal equivalent.
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Label opposite, adjacent, and hypotenuse relative to the given angle.

slides.title: Solution
slides.html:
Solution ReviewStudy the reasoning

Read the steps carefully, then summarize the method in your own words.

✦
Your turnWork it out on paper first — reveal when you're ready.

Solution:

  • The center of the circle is at \((2,2)\), and the radius is \(5\).
  • The tangent line at point \((5,6)\) is perpendicular to the radius at that point.
  • The slope of the radius is:
    \[ \frac{6-2}{5-2} = \frac{4}{3}. \]
  • Therefore, the slope of line \(m\) is the negative reciprocal:
    \[ -\frac{3}{4}. \]
  • Using the point-slope form:
    \[ y - 6 = -\frac{3}{4}(x - 5). \]
  • Setting \(x = 0\) to find the \(y\)-intercept:
    \[ y = 6 + \frac{15}{4} = \frac{24}{4} + \frac{15}{4} = \frac{39}{4}. \]
  • Therefore:
    \[ \boxed{\frac{39}{4}}. \]
slides.study_tip: Walk through each step and ask: why is this move valid? Could you explain it to a friend?

slides.title: Question 3
slides.html:
Strategy

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

The expression

\[ 6x^4 + 17x^2 + 7 \]

If side \(QR\) is extended through point \(R\) to point \(S\), and the measure of angle \(PRS\) is \((x + y)^\circ\), what is the value of \(x + y\)?

checkpoint.title: Question 12
checkpoint.stem_html:
Strategy

Label opposite, adjacent, and hypotenuse relative to the given angle.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

The manager of a gym selected a sample of 139 members at random to estimate the percentage of the gym's members that would continue to pay for a membership if the price increased.

From the survey, the manager estimates that 82% of the gym's members would continue to pay for a membership if the price increased, with an associated margin of error of 6.39%.

If the survey is repeated with a random sample of 278 members and the results are calculated in the same way, which of the following will be the most likely effect of using the larger random sample compared to the smaller random sample?

choices.text: The margin of error will be lower.
The margin of error will be higher.
The estimate of the percentage of the gym's members that would continue to pay for a membership if the price increased will be lower.
The estimate of the percentage of the gym's members that would continue to pay for a membership if the price increased will be higher.

The mass of object A is 483% of the mass of object B, and the mass of object A is 0.069% of the mass of object C.

If the mass of object C is \(p\%\) of the mass of object B, what is the value of \(\dfrac{p}{1,\!000}\)?

checkpoint.title: Question 15
checkpoint.stem_html:
Strategy

Mark known angles and side relationships first, then use triangle or parallel-line facts.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

For triangle \(PQR\) and triangle \(STU\), angles \(P\) and \(S\) each measure \(30^\circ\), \(QR = 16\), and \(TU = 12\).

Which additional piece(s) of information is(are) sufficient to prove that triangle \(PQR\) is similar to triangle \(STU\)?

  • I \(\angle Q = 40^\circ\) and \(\angle U = 110^\circ\)
  • II \(\dfrac{PR}{SU} = \dfrac{4}{3}\)

choices.text: I is sufficient, but II is not.
II is sufficient, but I is not.
I is sufficient, and II is sufficient.
Neither I nor II is sufficient.

Strategy

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

A real estate company offers a series of three webinars. 2,000 people attended the first webinar. 65% of the people who attended the first webinar attended the second webinar, and 44% of the people who attended both the first and second webinars attended the third webinar.

If those who attended the first but did not attend the second webinar, 31% attended the third webinar.

How many people attended both the first and third webinars but did not attend the second webinar?

Hard Question Set 9

slides.title: Question 1
slides.html:
Strategy

Label opposite, adjacent, and hypotenuse relative to the given angle.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

Elizabeth has $4,500 in an account. Each year she expects to have 11% more money in the account than the previous year.

Which of the following models best describes how Elizabeth expects the money in her account to change over time?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: For multiple choice, eliminate obviously wrong options before doing full algebra.
slides.strategy_hint: Label opposite, adjacent, and hypotenuse relative to the given angle.

slides.title: Answer 1
slides.html:
Correct Answer
C

slides.title: Question 2
slides.html:
Strategy

Mark known angles and side relationships first, then use triangle or parallel-line facts.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

The function

\[ T(x) = \frac{20,000 - 6.5x}{1,000} \]

gives the estimated air temperature \(T(x)\), in degrees Celsius (\(^\circ\text{C}\)), at an altitude of \(x\) meters.

If the estimated temperature surrounding the hot air balloon is \(15^\circ\text{C}\), which of the following is closest to the altitude \(x\) of the hot air balloon?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: For multiple choice, eliminate obviously wrong options before doing full algebra.
slides.strategy_hint: Mark known angles and side relationships first, then use triangle or parallel-line facts.

slides.title: Answer 2
slides.html:
Correct Answer
B

slides.title: Question 3
slides.html:
Strategy

Label opposite, adjacent, and hypotenuse relative to the given angle.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

The function \(f\) is defined as:

\[ f(x) = (x - a)(x - b) \]

where \(a\) and \(b\) are integer constants.

Given that:

\[f(17) > 0, f(20) \lt 0, f(23) > 0\]

What is one possible value of \(a + b\)?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Label opposite, adjacent, and hypotenuse relative to the given angle.

slides.title: Answer 3
slides.html:
Correct Answer
A

slides.title: Question 4
slides.html:
Strategy

Translate the percent change into a multiplier, then match the time unit in the exponent.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

The positive number \(a\) is 2,106% of the sum of the positive numbers \(b\) and \(c\), and \(b\) is 81% of \(c\).

What percent of \(b\) is \(a\)?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Translate the percent change into a multiplier, then match the time unit in the exponent.

slides.title: Answer 4
slides.html:
Correct Answer
A

slides.title: Question 5
slides.html:
Strategy

Label opposite, adjacent, and hypotenuse relative to the given angle.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

For the function \(f\), for every increase of 2 in the value of \(x\), the value of \(f(x)\) increases by a factor of \(c\), where \(c\) is a constant.

Which of the following equivalent forms of function \(f\) displays the value of \(c\) as the base or the coefficient?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: For multiple choice, eliminate obviously wrong options before doing full algebra.
slides.strategy_hint: Label opposite, adjacent, and hypotenuse relative to the given angle.

slides.title: Answer 5
slides.html:
Correct Answer
D

slides.title: Question 6
slides.html:
Strategy

Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

A study investigated the effect of magnesium supplementation on sleep quality. 190 adults were randomly selected, and their sleep efficiency was measured.

Participants were randomly assigned to take a magnesium supplement or a placebo for 6 weeks. The study concluded that magnesium improved sleep quality.

What feature of this study supports this conclusion?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Identify what type of linear equation this is (one solution, none, or infinite) before solving.

slides.title: Answer 6
slides.html:
Correct Answer
D

slides.title: Question 7
slides.html:
Strategy

Label opposite, adjacent, and hypotenuse relative to the given angle.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

A circle in the \(xy\)-plane has its center at \((-1, 1)\). Line \(t\) is tangent to this circle at the point \((7, -6)\).

Which of the following points also lies on line \(t\)?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: For multiple choice, eliminate obviously wrong options before doing full algebra.
slides.strategy_hint: Label opposite, adjacent, and hypotenuse relative to the given angle.

slides.title: Answer 7
slides.html:
Correct Answer
C

slides.title: Question 8
slides.html:
Strategy

Label opposite, adjacent, and hypotenuse relative to the given angle.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

The function \(f(x)\) is defined by:

\[ f(x) = \frac{x^2 + ax + b}{2x + c} \]

where \(a\), \(b\), and \(c\) are constants.

The graph of \(f(x)\) does not intersect the line \(x = 4\). If \(f(5) = f(6) = 0\), what is the value of \(a + b + c\)?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: Write your full work before opening the solution — partial credit thinking helps on test day.
slides.strategy_hint: Label opposite, adjacent, and hypotenuse relative to the given angle.

slides.title: Answer 8
slides.html:
Correct Answer
B

slides.title: Question 9
slides.html:
Strategy

Boundary first: rewrite as an equation, then pick a test point to decide which side to shade.

Question PracticeTry it first

Pause and solve before checking the answer or worked solution.

The shaded region represents the solutions to the inequality:

\[ rx + ty \geq -65 \]

What is the value of \(r + t\)?

Choose your answer
slides.study_tip: When you multiply or divide by a negative, flip the inequality sign — then check with a test value.
slides.strategy_hint: Boundary first: rewrite as an equation, then pick a test point to decide which side to shade.

slides.title: Answer 9
slides.html:
Correct Answer
A

A study investigated the effect of magnesium supplementation on sleep quality. 190 adults were randomly selected, and their sleep efficiency was measured.

choices.text: There were more than 100 participants in the study.
The participants were selected at random from the community center.
The sleep efficiency of each participant was measured again at the end of 6 weeks.
Each participant was randomly assigned to take a magnesium supplement or a placebo.
